review: FEBRUARY 27, 1969 — FILLMORE WEST, SAN FRANCISCO. THE BUILDING HAD BEEN THE CAROUSEL BALLROOM — A UTOPIAN COLLECTIVE RUN BY THE DEAD, JEFFERSON AIRPLANE, QUICKSILVER, AND BIG BROTHER UNTIL THEY FELL SIXTY-SIX THOUSAND DOLLARS IN DEBT. BILL GRAHAM TOOK IT OVER AND RENAMED IT. THE CEILING WAS DRAPED IN "VELVET SILVER GLITTERY FABRIC ARRANGED LIKE HUGE UPSIDE-DOWN MUSHROOMS." THIS IS THE FIRST NIGHT OF THE FOUR-NIGHT RUN THAT PRODUCED LIVE/DEAD — ROCK'S FIRST SIXTEEN-TRACK LIVE RECORDING. THE TWENTY-THREE-MINUTE DARK STAR FROM THIS NIGHT BECAME THE CENTERPIECE OF THE ALBUM. PENTANGLE OPENED — GARCIA LATER SAID HEARING THEM PLANTED THE SEED FOR THE DEAD'S OWN ACOUSTIC FORMAT. VILLAGE VOICE: "THE FINEST ROCK IMPROVISATION EVER RECORDED." THE RECORDING WAS MADE ON PROTOTYPE NUMBER TWO OF THE AMPEX MM-1000 SIXTEEN-TRACK MACHINE, PARKED IN A TRUCK ON THE STREET BECAUSE THE DECK WAS TOO HEAVY TO GET INSIDE THE BUILDING. SEVEN MUSICIANS ON STAGE — GARCIA, WEIR, LESH, KREUTZMANN, MICKEY HART, PIGPEN ON THE HAMMOND B-3, AND TOM CONSTANTEN ON THE VOX CONTINENTAL, WHO'D BEEN INSERTING COMBS AND DUTCH DIMES INTO PIANO STRINGS ON THE STUDIO RECORDS.

FEBRUARY 28, 1969 — FILLMORE WEST. NIGHT TWO. DICK LATVALA — THE MAN WHO CURATED THE BAND'S TAPE VAULT FOR SIX YEARS — SAID THIS WAS "THE BEST AND MOST EXCITING GRATEFUL DEAD SHOW EVER. WITHOUT A DOUBT." NONE OF IT WAS USED ON LIVE/DEAD, WHICH MAKES IT THE GREATEST DEAD SHOW NEVER OFFICIALLY RELEASED. THE SECOND SET IS ONE CONTINUOUS PIECE: CRYPTICAL ENVELOPMENT INTO THE OTHER ONE INTO DARK STAR INTO ST. STEPHEN INTO THE ELEVEN INTO ALLIGATOR INTO CAUTION INTO FEEDBACK INTO AND WE BID YOU GOODNIGHT — EVERY SONG DISSOLVING INTO THE NEXT. ONE REVIEWER: "IT LEFT ME STARING INTO THE REFRIGERATOR LIKE YOU DO SOMETIMES, NOT KNOWING WHAT I'D JUST HEARD." ANOTHER: "THE ALLIGATOR > CAUTION SEQUENCE DOESN'T EVER WANT TO END." THE FULL BOX SET COSTS THREE HUNDRED AND FIFTY DOLLARS. ONE BUYER: "WORTH EVERY PENNY. PURE GOLD — EVERY NIGHT OF THE RUN."

MARCH 1, 1969 — FILLMORE WEST. NIGHT THREE. AN EYEWITNESS: "I WENT TO THIS SHOW AND IT WAS A LIFE CHANGER. NEVER THOUGHT ABOUT MUSIC THE SAME WAY AGAIN. DURING DARK STAR, I WAS LOOKING UP AT THE GOLD LAME CEILING AND IT FELT LIKE THE ROOM HAD ELEVATED AND WAS SPINNING OFF INTO SPACE LIKE A ROCKET SHIP." THE OTHER ONE OPENS THE SHOW — NOT THE SECOND SET, THE SHOW — AND IT'S DESCRIBED AS "ONE OF THE MOST FEROCIOUS PIECES OF MUSIC EVER PERFORMED, BY ANY BAND." COSMIC CHARLIE: "THE FIRST TWENTY-FIVE SECONDS ARE SOME OF THE ABSOLUTELY GREATEST PSYCHEDELIC GARAGE DEAD ON TAPE." PHIL: "ON FIRE. MATCHING JERRY NOTE FOR NOTE." PENTANGLE AND SIR DOUGLAS QUINTET WERE ON THE BILL — BUT SIR DOUGLAS CANCELLED AND WAS REPLACED BY FRUMIOUS BANDERSNATCH.

MARCH 2, 1969 — FILLMORE WEST. THE FINAL NIGHT. "DARK STAR IS COMPLEX AND AGILE, LIKE A GYMNAST RUNNING THROUGH QUADRATIC EQUATIONS ON THE PARALLEL BARS." TOM CONSTANTEN — THE CLASSICALLY TRAINED KEYBOARDIST — DELIVERED "HIS BEST PLAYING I'VE HEARD, SWIRLING ORGAN" THROUGHOUT THE SECOND SET. THE RECORDING WAS MADE ON A PROTOTYPE SIXTEEN-TRACK MACHINE PARKED IN A TRUCK ON THE STREET BECAUSE THE DECK WAS TOO HEAVY TO GET INSIDE. FROM THESE FOUR NIGHTS, FIVE SONGS BECAME LIVE/DEAD — WHICH HIT GOLD AND PAID DOWN THE DEBT FROM THE EXPENSIVE AOXOMOXOA SESSIONS. LENNY KAYE IN ROLLING STONE: IT "FORESHADOWED WHERE ROCK IS LIKELY TO BE IN ABOUT FIVE YEARS." BLAIR JACKSON: THE BEST PSYCHEDELIC ROCK ALBUM OF THE SIXTIES. THREE HUNDRED AND FIFTY DOLLARS FOR THE BOX SET. "WORTH EVERY PENNY."

MAY 23, 1969 — SEMINOLE INDIAN VILLAGE, HOLLYWOOD, FLORIDA. THE BIG ROCK POW WOW — A THREE-DAY FESTIVAL ON SOVEREIGN SEMINOLE LAND, WHICH MEANT THERE WERE NO POLICE. TIMOTHY LEARY WAS THERE, CHANTING "TURN ON, TUNE IN, DROP OUT" FROM THE STAGE. ORANGE SUNSHINE WAS REPORTEDLY DISTRIBUTED THROUGH THE ORANGE JUICE. ONLY A FEW THOUSAND PEOPLE ATTENDED, CREATING AN INTIMACY THAT WOULD HAVE BEEN IMPOSSIBLE AT WOODSTOCK THREE MONTHS LATER. THE RESERVATION HAD BEEN ESTABLISHED IN 1926. ADJACENT TO THE STAGE WAS OKALEE VILLAGE — A SEMINOLE TOURIST ATTRACTION FAMOUS FOR ALLIGATOR WRESTLING. NATIVE DANCING AND CHANTS HAPPENED ONSTAGE AND OFF THROUGHOUT THE FESTIVAL, BLENDING INDIGENOUS CULTURE WITH PSYCHEDELIC ROCK IN A WAY THAT EXISTED NOWHERE ELSE. OWSLEY "BEAR" STANLEY RECORDED THE SHOW PROFESSIONALLY — HIS TAPES EVENTUALLY BECAME ROAD TRIPS VOLUME 4 NUMBER 1. POLICE WERE STATIONED OUTSIDE THE RESERVATION BORDERS, SEARCHING ANYONE WHO LEFT. MUDDY WATERS AND JOHNNY WINTER ALSO PLAYED. THE DEAD PERFORMED ON FRIDAY AND SATURDAY.

FEBRUARY 18, 1971 — CAPITOL THEATER, PORT CHESTER, NEW YORK. JERRY GARCIA ONCE SAID THERE WERE ONLY TWO THEATERS IN THE WHOLE COUNTRY SET UP RIGHT FOR MUSIC — THE FILLMORE EAST AND THIS ONE. THE BUILDING OPENED IN 1926 WITH A SIXTY-FIVE-FOOT DOMED CEILING AND PRESIDENTIAL BOXES. BY 1970, PROMOTER HOWARD STEIN HAD TRANSFORMED IT INTO "A PSYCHEDELIC HAVEN AT THE CENTER OF THE LIVE-MUSIC UNIVERSE." FIVE NEW SONGS DEBUTED THIS NIGHT: BERTHA, LOSER, GREATEST STORY EVER TOLD, PLAYING IN THE BAND, AND WHARF RAT. SEVERAL OF THESE BECAME PERMANENT STAPLES PLAYED FOR THE NEXT TWENTY-FOUR YEARS. THE "BEAUTIFUL JAM" EMBEDDED WITHIN A DARK STAR > WHARF RAT > DARK STAR SEQUENCE THAT NIGHT IS LEGENDARY. MICKEY HART HAD LEFT THE BAND DAYS EARLIER — HIS FATHER HAD EMBEZZLED A HUNDRED AND FIFTY-FIVE THOUSAND DOLLARS FROM THE DEAD AND MICKEY QUIT FROM SHAME. THIS WAS A FIVE-PIECE BAND NOW, WITH NO SECOND DRUMMER AND NO KEYBOARDIST. THE LEAN SOUND REVEALED THINGS THAT HAD BEEN HIDDEN — LESH SAID HE COULD FINALLY HEAR WEIR'S GUITAR CLEARLY AND WAS "ASTONISHED, DELIGHTED AND EXCITED BEYOND MEASURE AT WHAT BOBBY WAS DOING."

FEBRUARY 19, 1971 — CAPITOL THEATER. NIGHT TWO. "THE PIG-PEN NIGHT." THE EASY WIND WAS "CHUGGING ALONG LIKE A PYROTECHNIC FREIGHT TRAIN THROUGH A COMPLETELY COSMIC, HARMONICALLY PERFECT JAM." IT TOOK ONE FAN THIRTY YEARS TO FIND IT ONLINE AFTER LISTENING TO AN EVER-DETERIORATING CASSETTE COPY. DEAL AND BIRD SONG DEBUTED THIS NIGHT — THE SECOND NIGHT OF DEBUTS IN A ROW AFTER FIVE NEW SONGS THE NIGHT BEFORE. MICKEY HART'S SECOND-TO-LAST SHOW BEFORE HIS THREE-YEAR DEPARTURE. THE GOOD LOVIN' "EASILY HOLDS ITS OWN WITH THE MONSTER APRIL VERSIONS" — PIGPEN DELIVERING "AN INFECTIOUS R&B FLAVORED GROOVE." RELEASED AS THREE FROM THE VAULT.

FEBRUARY 20, 1971 — CAPITOL THEATER. NIGHT THREE. ONE OF ONLY THREE ELECTRIC PERFORMANCES OF RIPPLE IN THE DEAD'S ENTIRE CAREER — "A BEAUTIFUL AND EMOTIVE PERFORMANCE." THE GDTRFB CODA "SNEAKS UP ON YOU, SLOWLY AND EFFORTLESSLY EXTENDING AND MORPHING INTO SOMETHING TRULY REMARKABLE." THIS WAS "THE DEAD'S FINEST, LOVELIEST YEAR — LOTS OF ROOM FOR PHIL TO CONTRIBUTE." EYEWITNESS: "SOMETIMES THE RECORDINGS DON'T MATCH THE LIVE SHOW, BUT SOME OF US KNOW ABOUT THE INTERMINABLE TUNING, PHIL SPINNING HIS CORD, JERRY BOBBING."

FEBRUARY 21, 1971 — CAPITOL THEATER. NIGHT FOUR. "AN IMPORTANT CROSSROADS BETWEEN THE NEW DEAD WITH LOSER, SUGAR MAG, BIRD SONG, PLAYIN', TRUCKIN', AS THEY DECOUPLED FROM THE DARK STAR > LOVELIGHT > NFA ERA." EYEWITNESS: "FIRST SHOW WITHOUT MICKEY, BUT HIS DRUMS WERE STILL ON STAGE." ANOTHER EYEWITNESS DROVE UP FROM PHILLY WITH HIS SISTER FOR THIS ONE — HIS FIRST DEAD SHOW.

FEBRUARY 23, 1971 — CAPITOL THEATER. NIGHT FIVE. EVAN S. HUNT: "BLUE RON ON THE HAMMOND B-3, SOUNDING PRETTY GOOD TO ME. THE GUY WAS DYING, AND HERE, GIVING US EVERYTHING HE HAD." PHIL WAS "A FULL-TIME SINGER" FOR THE LAST TIME — BY 1972 HE'D RETREATED FROM THE MICROPHONE. THE TRUCKIN' > DRUMS > OTHER ONE > WHARF RAT SEQUENCE WAS PERFORMED HERE FOR THE FIRST TIME — A COMBINATION THAT WOULD BECOME A SECOND-SET STAPLE FOR YEARS.

FEBRUARY 24, 1971 — CAPITOL THEATER. THE FINAL NIGHT. SIX SHOWS IN SEVEN DAYS. THEY TURNED PIGPEN LOOSE — THE HARD TO HANDLE WAS HOT, BUT THE LONG GOOD LOVIN' WAS EVEN BETTER. A GREAT KING BEE — ONE OF ONLY SEVEN IN 1971. AND A LOVELIGHT TO BRING THE HOUSE DOWN. THE TRUCKIN' > NFA > GDTRFB > NFA "HAS TO BE EXPERIENCED TO BE BELIEVED." EYEWITNESS: "I REMEMBER A PIGPEN AND JANIS JOPLIN DUET ON LOVELIGHT — MOST OF MY TAPES WERE EITHER FILLMORE EAST OR PORT CHESTER." JANIS HAD BEEN DEAD FOR FOUR MONTHS.

APRIL 4, 1971 — MANHATTAN CENTER, NEW YORK. OSCAR HAMMERSTEIN BUILT THIS BUILDING IN 1906 AS AN OPERA HOUSE TO COMPETE WITH THE MET. THE MET PAID HIM ONE POINT TWO MILLION DOLLARS TO STOP. THE SEVENTH-FLOOR BALLROOM HAD A HAND-PAINTED CEILING MURAL AND HOSTED THE FIRST FILM WITH A RECORDED SOUNDTRACK IN 1926. BILLED AS THE DEAD'S "FIRST ANNUAL DANCE MARATHON" — FIVE DOLLARS GENERAL ADMISSION, SOLD OUT. BY FOUR O'CLOCK THE STREETS WERE FLOODED. A CASE WESTERN RESERVE REVIEWER: "ABOUT FIVE THOUSAND PEOPLE IN A BALLROOM THAT HELD TWO THOUSAND. THERE WASN'T A SOUL SITTING STILL." LACED ORANGES WERE THROWN TO THE CROWD FROM THE STAGE. AN EYEWITNESS: "MY FIRST DEAD SHOW AND MY FIRST ROCK CONCERT. I WAS SIXTEEN AND HAD MY LITTLE MIND THOROUGHLY BLOWN." GARCIA'S GUITAR "SOUNDS LIKE TENNESSEE WHISKEY." AFTER SIX HOURS, UNCLE JOHN'S BAND AND THE END — "A FULL HALF HOUR AFTER THE CONCERT, HALF THE CROWD WAS STILL THERE WAITING FOR MORE."

APRIL 5, 1971 — MANHATTAN CENTER. NIGHT TWO. "PIG IS SOULFUL, IN RARE FORM ON KING BEE AND LOVELIGHT. THE TRUCKIN' > OTHER ONE MEDLEY HAS THAT OLD 1969 FLAVOR OF INTENSITY AND COLOR." THE NOT FADE AWAY > GOIN' DOWN THE ROAD > NOT FADE AWAY FROM THIS NIGHT IS THE VERSION USED ON SKULL AND ROSES. GARCIA CALLED THAT ALBUM "THE PROTOTYPE GRATEFUL DEAD — WE'RE JUST A REGULAR SHOOT-EM-UP SALOON BAND." EYEWITNESS: "ANGEL CHICKS WERE WALKING UP AND DOWN THE AISLES AND TOLD PEOPLE TAKE A SIP AND PASS IT ON. WORD IS IT WAS OWSLEY OJ." DURING LOVELIGHT: "IT WAS ONLY A RED SPOTLIGHT ON PIG AND I SWEAR IT WAS LIKE DANTE'S INFERNO."

APRIL 6, 1971 — MANHATTAN CENTER. THE THIRD AND FINAL NIGHT. THIS IS THE SKULL AND ROSES PLAYING IN THE BAND AND HOG FOR YOU. MERL SAUNDERS OVERDUBBED SOME ORGAN FOR THE FINAL RELEASE — BUT THE ORIGINAL, WITHOUT THE OVERDUB, IS HERE.

APRIL 7, 1971 — BOSTON MUSIC HALL. FIRST OF TWO NIGHTS IN THE OLDEST CONCERT HALL IN BOSTON — BUILT IN 1852 WITH THE FIRST PIPE ORGAN IN THE COUNTRY. "APRIL 1971 IS ON PAR WITH MAY 1977" — THE DEAD WERE ON A ROLL COMING OFF PORT CHESTER AND MANHATTAN CENTER. THE SAINT STEPHEN IS RARE EVEN FOR EARLY 1971 — AFTER THIS SHOW, THE DEAD ONLY PLAYED STEPHEN TEN MORE TIMES ALL YEAR, AND FOUR WERE DURING THIS APRIL RUN.

APRIL 8, 1971 — BOSTON MUSIC HALL. NIGHT TWO. THE FIRST DARK STAR IN A WHILE — DEADBASE PUTS IT AS THE NUMBER FOURTEEN SHOW OF 1971. THE SECOND SET RUNS INTO A DARK STAR > SAINT STEPHEN > NOT FADE AWAY SEQUENCE THAT ONE REVIEWER COMPARED DIRECTLY TO THE FAMOUS 1977 CORNELL VERSION.

APRIL 25, 1971 — FILLMORE EAST, SECOND AVENUE AND SIXTH STREET, NEW YORK CITY. BILL GRAHAM'S "CHURCH OF ROCK AND ROLL." TWENTY-SEVEN HUNDRED SEATS IN A FORMER YIDDISH THEATER ON WHAT HAD BEEN THE "JEWISH RIALTO." ANDY WARHOL ONCE CONSIDERED TAKING IT OVER FOR THE VELVET UNDERGROUND BUT DECLINED. THE DEAD WERE HERE FOR THEIR FINAL FIVE NIGHTS — GRAHAM WAS CLOSING BOTH FILLMORES IN JUNE. THE FORMAT WAS TWO SHOWS A NIGHT AT EIGHT AND ELEVEN, TICKETS NEVER MORE THAN FIVE DOLLARS AND FIFTY CENTS. THE CONCESSION STAND SOLD BAGELS WITH CREAM CHEESE AND DANNON YOGURT. NEXT DOOR, RATNER'S KOSHER DELICATESSEN SERVED AS GRAHAM'S MAKESHIFT OFFICE. THE DEAD HAD ONCE DOSED GRAHAM'S SEVEN-UP WITH LSD BEFORE A SHOW AT THIS VENUE. GRAHAM — A GERMAN-BORN JEWISH REFUGEE WHO'D ESCAPED THE HOLOCAUST VIA THE RED CROSS, SERVED IN KOREA AND WON A BRONZE STAR AND PURPLE HEART — FORGAVE THEM. THE JOSHUA LIGHT SHOW PROVIDED THE VISUALS: FIVE PEOPLE USING FILM PROJECTORS, COLOR WHEELS, WATER AND OIL COLORS, AND GLASS CRYSTALS. THEIR WORK APPEARED ON ALBUM COVERS FOR HENDRIX'S BAND OF GYPSYS.

APRIL 26, 1971 — FILLMORE EAST. NIGHT TWO. DUANE ALLMAN SAT IN ON THREE SONGS — HIS SLIDE GUITAR ON SUGAR MAGNOLIA WAS PARTICULARLY DEVASTATING. PIGPEN, DESPITE DECLINING HEALTH, DELIVERED WHAT MULTIPLE REVIEWERS CONSIDER HIS FINEST HARD TO HANDLE. BUT THE HEADLINE WAS ELSEWHERE IN THE BUILDING. BOB DYLAN WAS WATCHING FROM THE SOUND BOOTH. ACCORDING TO ROLLING STONE, WHEN THE LIGHT SHOW BRIEFLY FLASHED THE WORD "DYLAN," HIS PRIVACY JEOPARDIZED, BOB SPLIT OUT THE DOOR. HIS REVIEW, DELIVERED TO NO ONE IN PARTICULAR, WAS TWO SENTENCES: "FUCK, THEY'RE DAMNED GOOD."

APRIL 27, 1971 — FILLMORE EAST. NIGHT THREE. THE BEACH BOYS SHOWED UP. GARCIA, WEIR, AND MIKE LOVE, CARL WILSON, BRUCE JOHNSTON, AND AL JARDINE JAMMED FOR FORTY-FIVE MINUTES ON JOHNNY B. GOODE, SEARCHIN', AND — IMPROBABLY — OKIE FROM MUSKOGEE. GARCIA WAS YELLING "FUN, FUN, FUN!" AT ONE POINT. FIRST AND ONLY PERFORMANCES OF RIOT IN CELL BLOCK NUMBER NINE AND HELP ME RHONDA. THE PREVIOUS NIGHT, DYLAN HAD WATCHED FROM THE SOUND BOOTH. TONIGHT THE BEACH BOYS WERE ON STAGE. THE FILLMORE EAST WAS CLOSING IN TWO MONTHS AND EVERYONE SEEMED TO KNOW IT — THE LAST DAYS OF A VENUE WHERE HENDRIX HAD RECORDED BAND OF GYPSYS AND THE CONCESSION STAND SOLD YOGURT AND BAGELS WITH CREAM CHEESE.

APRIL 28, 1971 — FILLMORE EAST. NIGHT FOUR. TOM CONSTANTEN — THE CLASSICALLY TRAINED KEYBOARDIST WHO'D LEFT THE BAND IN JANUARY 1970, THE ONE WHO'D INSERTED COMBS AND DUTCH DIMES INTO PIANO STRINGS — SAT IN FROM SUGAR MAGNOLIA THROUGH THE REST OF THE SECOND SET. THE HARD TO HANDLE IS WHAT PEOPLE TALK ABOUT. ONE REVIEWER: "IF THIS SHOW DOESN'T STEAL YOUR FACE AND THEN MELT IT LIKE BUTTER IN A MICROWAVE, SOMEONE NEEDS TO BEGIN CPR ON YOU BECAUSE RIGOR MORTIS CAN'T BE FAR AWAY." SOMEONE CREATED AN ARCHIVE.ORG ACCOUNT FOR THE SOLE PURPOSE OF SAYING HOW MUCH IT ROCKS. A FAN WHO ENTERED A CONTEST TO PICK THE ULTIMATE DEAD SET EVER PLAYED CHOSE THE SECOND SET FROM THIS NIGHT — AND WON. THE SUN WAS COMING UP WHEN PEOPLE LEFT THE THEATER.

APRIL 29, 1971 — FILLMORE EAST. THE FINAL NIGHT. BLAIR JACKSON: "THE SILKY BLUE POT SMOKE HAZE, HANGING IN THE AIR LIKE GAUZE MOSQUITO NETTING, BEING PIERCED BY GUITAR NOTES, SHARP AS ARROWS, AND BLOWN INTO SMALL TORNADOES OF DANCING LIGHT BY SHARP DRUM CRACKS AND THE FAT LIQUID THUMPING OF PHIL LESH'S BASS." THE MORNING DEW FROM THIS NIGHT IS CALLED "THE HEAVIEST AND DEEPEST" EVER PLAYED. THE COMMUNICATION AMONG THE CORE FOUR IS "ASTONISHING — IT'S THE GROUP MIND AT ITS HIGHEST AND MOST EXPRESSIVE." BILL GRAHAM MADE SURPRISE TRIPS TO THE MICROPHONE. THE ENCORE: UNCLE JOHN'S BAND AND AND WE BID YOU GOODNIGHT. AN EYEWITNESS: "AND THEN WHEN IT WAS ALL OVER, WE ALL SAT BACK AND SAID THANX BILL." THE FILLMORE EAST WOULD CLOSE FOR GOOD IN TWO MONTHS. THE ALLMAN BROTHERS PLAYED THE FINAL SHOW UNTIL DAWN.

AUGUST 6, 1971 — HOLLYWOOD PALLADIUM, SUNSET BOULEVARD. PURE STREAMLINE MODERNE ART DECO — AN ELEVEN-THOUSAND-TWO-HUNDRED-SQUARE-FOOT DANCE FLOOR THAT COULD HOLD FOUR THOUSAND. OPENING NIGHT IN 1940: TOMMY DORSEY AND HIS ORCHESTRA WITH FRANK SINATRA AS THE BAND VOCALIST. THE MOVIE STARS CAME TO SWING. THIRTY-ONE YEARS LATER, THE GRATEFUL DEAD. KREUTZMANN WAS THE SOLE DRUMMER. PIGPEN WAS STILL ON STAGE BUT FADING. KEITH GODCHAUX HADN'T JOINED YET — THAT WAS TWO MONTHS AWAY. THIS WAS THE LEANEST VERSION OF THE BAND: FIVE MUSICIANS ON A DANCE FLOOR BUILT FOR BIG BANDS, IN A ROOM WHERE SINATRA HAD ONCE SUNG FOR HIS SUPPER.

DECEMBER 14, 1971 — HILL AUDITORIUM, ANN ARBOR. DESIGNED BY ALBERT KAHN — THE MAN WHO BUILT FORD'S ROUGE RIVER PLANT — AND HAILED WHEN IT OPENED IN 1913 AS "A MONUMENT TO PERFECT ACOUSTICS." THE AUDITORIUM IS SHAPED LIKE A MEGAPHONE, WITH A PARABOLIC CEILING THAT MAKES THE SOUND BLOOM IN A WAY FEW ROOMS IN AMERICA CAN MATCH. THIRTY-FIVE HUNDRED SEATS. KEITH GODCHAUX HAD JOINED THE BAND LESS THAN TWO MONTHS EARLIER. DONNA JEAN HAD TOLD GARCIA AT A CLUB: "KEITH IS YOUR NEXT PIANO PLAYER." GARCIA GAVE HER HIS HOME PHONE NUMBER. KEITH ARRIVED AT A SUNDAY REHEARSAL WHERE THE BAND HAD CANCELLED WITHOUT TELLING JERRY — SO THEY PLAYED ALONE. LESH: "ALL THROUGH THE AFTERNOON, KEITH NEVER PUT A FOOT WRONG." KREUTZMANN: "HE COULD LEARN SONGS BEFORE HE WAS EVEN DONE HEARING THEM FOR THE FIRST TIME." THE NEXT DAY HE WAS ON THE PAYROLL. HE HAD NEVER PLAYED ROCK AND ROLL PROFESSIONALLY.

DECEMBER 15, 1971 — HILL AUDITORIUM, ANN ARBOR. NIGHT TWO. "MONUMENT TO PERFECT ACOUSTICS." THE LOVELIGHT IS ONE OF THE LAST — PIGPEN "LETS IT ALL HANG OUT." SOMEONE YELLS SOMETHING AND JERRY RESPONDS: "WHAT'S THAT? YOU WANT THE PIANO LOUDER?" THEREAFTER, KEITH IS LOUDER. THE DARK STAR > DEAL TRANSITION IS A ONE-TIME-ONLY COMBINATION. THE STARS AND STRIPES FOREVER APPEARS IN THE TUNING — A CHRISTMAS-SEASON JOKE. EYEWITNESS: "WHAT A SHOW. HILL AUDITORIUM IS AN AMAZING VENUE. I'VE SEEN THE DEAD MANY TIMES INCLUDING THE FIRST TOUR IN 1967 AT THE GRANDE BALLROOM. NONE COMPARE WITH THIS ONE." ANOTHER REVIEWER: "DECEMBER 1971 IS SUPERIOR TO EVERYTHING ON EUROPE '72." RELEASED AS DAVE'S PICKS VOLUME 26.

MARCH 21, 1972 — ACADEMY OF MUSIC. NIGHT ONE OF SEVEN. "THE DRESS REHEARSAL FOR THE EUROPE TOUR." A DILAPIDATED THREE-THOUSAND-SEAT MOVIE HOUSE WHERE "PEOPLE DANCED IN THE AISLES" AND "IT COULD BE LOUD AND PEOPLE LOVED IT LOUD." EYEWITNESS: "SAT IN THE ORCHESTRA AT THE FUNKY FAR-OUT ACADEMY. MIRRORED BALL TWISTING AND TURNING DURING THE OTHER ONE AND WHEN THE BOYS VENTURED INTO DEEP SPACE. AFTER THESE SHOWS THERE WAS NO TURNING BACK. MY SEAT WAS SECURED ON THE BUS." THE FIRST TRULY EXTENDED PLAYING IN THE BAND JAM — WHERE THEY GO "UNBRIDLED STRATOSPHERIC" AFTER THE VOCAL VERSES — HAPPENED DURING THIS RUN.

MARCH 22, 1972 — ACADEMY OF MUSIC, NEW YORK. NIGHT TWO OF SEVEN. THE HELL'S ANGELS BENEFIT. THE DEAD PLAYED BACKUP FOR BO DIDDLEY — "RANGED IN A GRINNING LINE BEHIND BIG BO'S BLACK SILK BULK, THE BEST BAND HE'S EVER LIKELY TO PLAY WITH." DONNA JEAN GODCHAUX'S FIRST OFFICIAL APPEARANCE AS A MEMBER OF THE BAND. BEFORE THE SHOW, GARCIA: "IT'S REALLY FAR OUT, JUST TOO FUCKIN' NEAT, MAN... WE'VE ONLY JUST STARTED GETTIN' INTO WHAT WE CAN DO. THERE'S NO LIMIT... AND WE'RE ALL FEELIN' GOOD." RELEASED AS PART OF DICK'S PICKS VOLUME 30.

MARCH 23, 1972 — ACADEMY OF MUSIC. NIGHT THREE. THE SOLE DARK STAR OF THE RUN. "PURE BEAUTIFUL GROUP IMPROV — SOUNDS LIKE COMPOSING A NEW SONG ON THE SPOT." EYEWITNESS: "OLD VAUDEVILLE HOUSE, FOUR OR FIVE BALCONIES, MIRROR BALL HANGING FROM THE CEILING, HIPPIES, FREAKS, WEIRDOS, DROP-OUTS, DRUGGIES AND OTHER COUNTER-CULTURE ELITE." JERRY HAD HIS PEDAL STEEL SET UP FOR THE ENTIRE RUN — THE LOOKS LIKE RAIN WITH STEEL "RIVALS ANY OF THE FINEST THAT WOULD FOLLOW IN EUROPE." PHIL SANG THE HARMONY PARTS THAT DONNA WOULD LATER TAKE. EYEWITNESS: "THE MEN'S ROOM WAS SO FILLED WITH SMOKE YOU DIDN'T HAVE TO LIGHT UP TO CATCH A BUZZ."

MARCH 25, 1972 — ACADEMY OF MUSIC. THE SATURDAY NIGHT SHOW — A HELL'S ANGELS BENEFIT WITH BO DIDDLEY ON THE BILL. THE DEAD BACKED HIM, "RANGED IN A GRINNING LINE BEHIND BIG BO'S BLACK SILK BULK." DONNA JEAN'S FIRST OFFICIAL APPEARANCES WITH THE BAND WERE THIS WEEK. EYEWITNESS: "PLAYED AT A HELLS ANGELS' PARTY! CAN ONLY IMAGINE THE SCENE THAT NIGHT."

MARCH 26, 1972 — ACADEMY OF MUSIC. NIGHT SIX. PHIL DOING HARMONIES INSTEAD OF DONNA ON LOOKS LIKE RAIN — WITH PEDAL STEEL. "THIS IS WHERE '72 STARTS SOUNDING AMAZING." EYEWITNESS: "LISTENING TO THIS SHOW ALMOST MADE ME FEEL LIKE I WAS TRIPPING. WHEN I HEARD GREATEST STORY FOR THE OPENER I KNEW THIS WOULD BE SPECIAL."

MARCH 27, 1972 — ACADEMY OF MUSIC. NIGHT SEVEN. THE LAST AMERICAN SHOW BEFORE EUROPE. "THE BAND IS LOOSE. JERRY IS SINGING SO HARD HE IS OVERPOWERING HIS MICROPHONE." EYEWITNESS: "PIG HAD THE CROWD IN HIS POCKET. BOSS MAN HAD TO BE WRITTEN FOR HIM." EVERY TIME PIGPEN GOT TO "YA LIED, CHEATED" — "THE WHOLE CROWD WOULD JOIN IN." IN TEN DAYS THEY'D BE AT WEMBLEY.

MARCH 28, 1972 — ACADEMY OF MUSIC. THE FINAL NIGHT. PATRICK CARR: "THE BEST SET I HAVE EVER HEARD" WITH "EVERY NOTE IN PLACE." JERRY BEFORE THE SHOW: "IT'S REALLY FAR OUT, JUST TOO FUCKIN' NEAT, MAN... WE'VE ONLY JUST STARTED GETTIN' INTO WHAT WE CAN DO." PIGPEN'S HARMONICA "RENDERING SWEEPING BLUES, BLOWING EVERYTHING FROM HIS TINY EMACIATED FRAME DOWN INTO HIS WAILING HARP." "SIDEWALKS OF NEW YORK" — A BRIEF INSTRUMENTAL, PLAYED ONCE AND NEVER AGAIN. EYEWITNESS: "AFTER THESE SHOWS THERE WAS NO TURNING BACK."

APRIL 8, 1972 — WEMBLEY EMPIRE POOL, LONDON. BUILT FOR THE 1934 BRITISH EMPIRE GAMES BY AN ENGINEER WHO DESIGNED IT WITHOUT AN ARCHITECT — ENTIRELY REINFORCED CONCRETE AND GLASS, NO STEEL, WITH THE WIDEST CONCRETE SPAN IN THE WORLD. THE SWIMMING POOL WAS TWO HUNDRED FEET LONG AND HAD BEEN USED FOR THE 1948 OLYMPICS. IT WAS NEVER USED FOR SWIMMING AGAIN. THE ACOUSTICS WERE NOTORIOUS. BANDS' CREWS HUNG PARACHUTE SILK FROM THE CEILING TO TAME THE ECHOES BOUNCING AROUND THE CONCRETE CAVERN. LED ZEPPELIN HAD BEEN THE FIRST ROCK BAND HERE JUST FIVE MONTHS EARLIER. THE GUARDIAN SAID IT TOOK AN HOUR BEFORE "THE QUALITY OF THE DEAD'S PLAYING COULD BE APPRECIATED" — BUT THEN CALLED THEM "THE BEST LIVE BAND IN AMERICA." NIGHT TWO OF THE EUROPE '72 TOUR. THE TOURING PARTY: FORTY-THREE PEOPLE IN TWO BUSES — THE BOZOS AND THE BOLOS, NAMED FOR THEIR RESPECTIVE VEHICLES, A DIVIDE THAT MASKED REAL TENSIONS BETWEEN CREW AND ARTISTS.

APRIL 8, 1972 — WEMBLEY EMPIRE POOL, LONDON. A DIFFERENT TRANSFER OF THE SAME SHOW. THIS IS THE MILLER VERSION — SLIGHTLY WARMER THAN THE GILES TRANSFER, WITH MORE LOW-END DEFINITION IN LESH'S BASS. TWENTY-TWO SONGS OVER NEARLY THREE HOURS. PIGPEN'S SEVENTEEN-MINUTE CAUTION CLOSED THE SECOND SET WITH RAUNCHY HARMONICA AND A BLUES RAP THAT CHANNELED ELMORE JAMES. WEIR TOLD THE NOTORIOUS YELLOW DOG JOKE TO STALL WHILE SOMEONE FIXED A BROKEN STRING. THE CUMBERLAND BLUES FROM THIS NIGHT ENDED UP ON THE EUROPE '72 ALBUM — WITH GARCIA'S VOCAL SECRETLY OVERDUBBED IN THE STUDIO THAT JULY.

APRIL 26, 1972 — JAHRHUNDERTHALLE, FRANKFURT. A CHEMICAL COMPANY BUILT A CONCERT HALL AS A BIRTHDAY PRESENT TO ITSELF — HOECHST AG COMMISSIONED IT FOR THEIR HUNDREDTH ANNIVERSARY. A FREE-FLOATING GLASS DOME. WHEN JETHRO TULL PLAYED, TICKETLESS FANS BROKE THE DOME'S PANES. THE TRUCKIN' > OTHER ONE > COMES A TIME SEQUENCE "PRETTY MUCH MUSICALLY DEFINES BEING A DEADHEAD." RELEASED AS "HUNDRED YEAR HALL" — THE LITERAL ENGLISH TRANSLATION.

MAY 3, 1972 — OLYMPIA THEATRE, PARIS. THE OLDEST MUSIC HALL IN PARIS — OPENED IN 1893 BY THE CO-FOUNDER OF THE MOULIN ROUGE. FOUR HUNDRED METERS OF NEON TUBES. PIAF SANG "NON, JE NE REGRETTE RIEN" HERE WHILE GRAVELY ILL. THE BEATLES PLAYED EIGHTEEN DAYS BEFORE CONQUERING AMERICA. ROCK SCULLY: "TRADITION-RIDDLED PLACE. EVERYONE KEEPS SAYING HOW SPECIAL IT IS BECAUSE OF WHO'S PLAYED HERE." THE BAND HAD SPENT A FEW DAYS WANDERING PARIS "IN A CHEMICAL CLOUD, GOING TO THE LOUVRE." DONNA TOOK FIFTEEN HITS OF OWSLEY'S BEST — NOBODY TOLD HER THAT RAMROD HAD BROUGHT A FRESH BATCH AND THE OLD DOSAGE GUIDELINES NO LONGER APPLIED. THE SOUND QUALITY FROM THIS NIGHT IS "THE FINEST OF THE ENTIRE TOUR." THE TRUCKIN' > OTHER ONE SEQUENCE: "AVANTE-GARDE, LIKE MILES DAVIS'S LATE SIXTIES STUFF."

MAY 4, 1972 — OLYMPIA THEATRE, PARIS. NIGHT TWO. A PARISIAN EYEWITNESS: "I WAS EIGHTEEN. THIS WAS MY FIRST GRATEFUL DEAD CONCERT. I HAD NEVER HEARD ANYTHING LIKE THIS DARK STAR AND THIS MUSIC HAS TRAUMATIZED ME FOR ALL MY LIFE." THE DARK STAR HAS "AT LEAST SIX DISTINCT AND INSTANTLY RECOGNIZABLE MOVEMENTS." PIGPEN WAS STILL ON STAGE — BARELY. HE SANG TWO SOULS IN COMMUNION, AND "ANYONE WHO COULD HEAR IT AND NOT BE DEEPLY AND PROFOUNDLY MOVED" WASN'T PAYING ATTENTION. SONGS FROM THIS SHOW APPEAR ON FIVE DIFFERENT OFFICIAL RELEASES. THE SUGAR MAGNOLIA — USED ON EUROPE '72 — IS "THE PIECE DE RESISTANCE." THINK ABOUT THE AGES OF THIS BAND: JERRY TWENTY-NINE, BOB TWENTY-FOUR, PHIL THIRTY-TWO, PIGPEN TWENTY-SIX, KEITH TWENTY-THREE. IN SIX WEEKS PIGPEN WOULD PLAY HIS LAST SHOW.

MAY 7, 1972 — BICKERSHAW FESTIVAL, WIGAN, LANCASHIRE. A THREE-DAY OUTDOOR FESTIVAL ORGANIZED BY A MANCHESTER BUSINESSMAN, A WIGAN MARKET TRADER, AND — REMARKABLY — JEREMY BEADLE, BEFORE HE BECAME FAMOUS AS A BRITISH TELEVISION PRESENTER. FORTY TO SIXTY THOUSAND PEOPLE. IT RAINED BEFORE THE FESTIVAL OPENED AND THEN FOR MOST OF THE THREE DAYS. A TANK USED BY A HIGH-DIVE ACT WAS EMPTIED DIRECTLY ONTO THE AREA IN FRONT OF THE STAGE, CREATING A SEA OF MUD. THERE WERE FAT RATS. THE DEAD PLAYED A TWO-HUNDRED-AND-FORTY-FIVE-MINUTE SET — ABOUT FOUR HOURS — THE LARGEST AUDIENCE THEY PERFORMED FOR IN ALL OF THEIR EUROPEAN CAREER. BONFIRES RINGED THE FIELD, GIVING IT A PAGAN QUALITY. FIREWORKS WENT OFF DURING DARK STAR IN WHAT WITNESSES CALLED "A TRULY MAGIC MOMENT." PIGPEN DELIVERED A TWENTY-MINUTE GOOD LOVIN' CONSIDERED AMONG HIS FINEST. AN EIGHTEEN-YEAR-OLD NAMED ELVIS COSTELLO STOOD IN THE MUD AND DECIDED TO START A BAND. JOE STRUMMER — FUTURE FRONTMAN OF THE CLASH — CALLED BICKERSHAW HIS FAVORITE CONCERT EVER. THE FESTIVAL WAS NEVER REPEATED. FIFTY YEARS LATER, "ECHOES OF FABLES ABOUT IT" STILL CIRCULATE IN WIGAN PUB CHATTER.

MAY 26, 1972 — LYCEUM THEATRE, WELLINGTON STREET, LONDON. THE BUILDING DATES TO 1771. BRAM STOKER SPENT HIS ENTIRE ADULT CAREER AS THIS THEATER'S MANAGER — AND WHILE WORKING HERE, WROTE DRACULA. HIS BOSS, THE GREAT ACTOR SIR HENRY IRVING, IS WIDELY BELIEVED TO BE THE REAL-LIFE INSPIRATION FOR THE COUNT. WHEN STOKER EAGERLY STAGED A READING AND ASKED IRVING'S OPINION, IRVING'S ONE-WORD ANSWER WAS: "DREADFUL." THE FINAL NIGHT OF EUROPE '72. THE DEAD SEEMED "AT HOME IN THE INTIMATE WARMTH OF THE OLD-TIME LYCEUM WITH ITS DIM, SMOKEY LIGHTS." THE LONGEST FIRST SET OF THE ENTIRE TOUR — TWO HOURS. MUCH OF THE EUROPE '72 TRIPLE LIVE ALBUM WAS SELECTED FROM THIS PERFORMANCE. THE LYCEUM IS NOW HOME TO DISNEY'S THE LION KING, WHICH HAS BEEN RUNNING SINCE 1999.

JULY 18, 1972 — ROOSEVELT STADIUM, JERSEY CITY. NINETEEN THOUSAND FANS ON A TUESDAY NIGHT. EYEWITNESS: "THREE GUYS IN ARABIAN NIGHTS GEAR DID A BED-OF-NAILS ACT AT THE BREAK — ONE LIES DOWN, ANOTHER SETS A CONCRETE BLOCK ON HIS STOMACH, THE THIRD SWINGS THE SLEDGEHAMMER. BLOCK BREAKS, GUY STANDS UP. THE PLACE GOES NUTS." FIVE AND A HALF HOURS, SEVEN TILL TWELVE-THIRTY, FIREWORKS GOING OFF AT CASEY JONES. THE COMES A TIME IS "NOTHING SHORT OF A CHURCH SERVICE" — "YOU COULD HEAR A PIN DROP." THE DARK STAR "POKES INTO ALL SORTS OF INTERESTING PLACES — AFTER TWENTY-TWO MINUTES COMES BACK WITH A VENGEANCE." EYEWITNESS: "MY SISTER AND HER BOYFRIEND TRIED TO LEAVE AFTER CASEY JONES THINKING IT WAS OVER. I SAID 'WHAT THE HELL ARE YOU DOING?' 'LEAVING,' SHE SAID. 'IT'S OVER, RIGHT?' WRONG."

AUGUST 12, 1972 — SACRAMENTO MEMORIAL AUDITORIUM. NORTHERN ITALIAN ROMANESQUE, FIVE SHADES OF LOCALLY MADE BRICK, DESIGNED BY THE ARCHITECT OF SAN FRANCISCO CITY HALL. THE OTHER ONE > BLACK PETER > OTHER ONE SANDWICH IS A RARITY FOR 1972 — "BLACK PETER WAS NOT A COMMON SONG THAT YEAR." KEITH'S WAH-WAH PEDAL ON THE OTHER ONE "IS ABSOLUTELY TEARING SHIT UP — AMAZING." THIS SHOW "KICKS OFF THE REAL MAGIC PERIOD OF AUGUST-OCTOBER '72."

AUGUST 20, 1972 — SAN JOSE CIVIC AUDITORIUM. A WPA-ERA BUILDING IN SPANISH COLONIAL STYLE WITH NATIVE AMERICAN DESIGNS ON THE LOBBY'S PAINTED WOODEN BEAMS. DEMPSEY AND JOE LOUIS FOUGHT HERE. GARCIA MET WEIR AT A MUSIC STORE EIGHT MILES AWAY. THE TRUCKIN' > OTHER ONE IS "EPIC — NOT TO BE MISSED." THE STELLA BLUE "FINISHES VERY STRONG." "DEEP, DEEP, DEEP. YOU'RE GOING TO HEAR THE DEAD PLAYING AT THE PEAK OF THEIR POWERS."

AUGUST 21, 1972 — BERKELEY COMMUNITY THEATER. PHIL LESH'S HIGH SCHOOL. HE TRANSFERRED TO BERKELEY HIGH AS A SOPHOMORE FOR THE ADVANCED MUSIC COURSES AND PLAYED TRUMPET IN THE CONTEST-WINNING JAZZ BAND. WHEN THE DEAD PLAYED FOUR NIGHTS HERE IN AUGUST 1972, PHIL WAS PERFORMING IN HIS OLD SCHOOL AUDITORIUM. THE BASKETBALL FLOOR WAS DESIGNED TO BE SLIGHTLY SPRINGY FOR ATHLETES. WHEN THE DEADHEADS STARTED DANCING, THE FLOOR BEGAN UNDULATING, CAUSING THE SPEAKER TOWERS TO SWAY APPROXIMATELY TWO FEET AT THEIR TOPS. MORE THAN ONE PERSON MISTOOK THE SENSATION FOR THE EFFECTS OF DRUGS. BILL GRAHAM'S INTRODUCTION ON THE FINAL NIGHT: "HAVEN'T DONE THIS FOR A WHILE. WE'D LIKE TO INTRODUCE THE BOYS FOR THE PEOPLE WHO ARE VISITING FROM BOISE." RESERVED SEATING, THREE FIFTY TO FIVE FIFTY. TWENTY PERCENT OF THE AUDIENCE WERE REPEATERS WHO BOUGHT TICKETS EVERY NIGHT. THE BAND HAD JUST RETURNED FROM EUROPE '72 AND WAS AT A CREATIVE PEAK.

AUGUST 22, 1972 — BERKELEY COMMUNITY THEATER. NIGHT TWO AT PHIL LESH'S OLD HIGH SCHOOL. JERRY TO A HECKLER: "'OH, RELAX, WE'LL GET TO ALL THAT TOP 40 SHIT LATER.' CHECK OUT PHIL'S BASS SOLO IN OTHER ONE. NOTICE THAT YOU'VE SPILLED YOUR DRINK." THE USHERS GAVE UP MIDWAY THROUGH THE FIRST SET AND DISAPPEARED — THE SEATED CROWD WAS NOT GOING TO STAY SEATED. THE GDTRFB "STARTS VERY MELLOW, THEN JERRY JUST STARTS RIFLING OFF A DIZZYING FLUTTER OF WILD LICKS" INTO A "WILD, ULTRA-RARE, SLIDE-DRIVEN HEY BO DIDDLEY." THE FINAL SWEET SUSIE EVER PERFORMED.

AUGUST 24, 1972 — BERKELEY COMMUNITY THEATER. NIGHT THREE. THE DARK STAR > MORNING DEW: "NEAR PERFECT." "PHIL'S BOOPITY-BOOPING IN SUGAREE IS HILARIOUSLY ARTISTIC." MULTIPLE REVIEWERS ARGUE THIS AND THE FOLLOWING NIGHT ARE BETTER THAN THE VENETA SHOW THREE DAYS LATER — BUT VENETA SITS IN AN "UNENVIABLE POSITION" AS THE MOST FAMOUS SHOW IN THE BAND'S HISTORY. "BOBBY SAYS 'GET A LOAD OF THIS' — REFERRING TO THE IMPENDING SCREECHING DONNA DOES IN THE UPCOMING PLAYING IN THE BAND."

AUGUST 25, 1972 — BERKELEY COMMUNITY THEATER. THE FINAL NIGHT. AN EYEWITNESS SAT DIRECTLY NEXT TO OWSLEY STANLEY WHILE HE MIXED FRONT OF HOUSE. "THE MAN WAS IN PARTICULARLY GOOD CHEER. IN THAT ONE NIGHT HE TAUGHT ME AND SHOWED ME MORE ABOUT LIVE AUDIO MIXING THAN I HAVE LEARNED IN THE PAST THIRTY-FIVE YEARS." THE ACOUSTICS WERE "ABSOLUTELY IMMACULATE." ANOTHER EYEWITNESS: "OWSLEY GOT ME IN FREE BACKSTAGE. I WAS WORKING IN THE FEDERAL HALFWAY HOUSE WHERE HE CAME FRESH OUTTA THE JOINT BECAUSE I HAD REFUSED INDUCTION AND WAS DOING TWO YEARS OF ALTERNATE SERVICE." THE FRIEND OF THE DEVIL "MAY BE THE BEST ONE I'VE HEARD." MULTIPLE REVIEWERS CLAIM THIS SHOW IS "IN BETTER FORM THAN THE CELEBRATED VENETA SHOW TWO DAYS LATER." THE BASKETBALL FLOOR WAS STILL BOUNCING.

AUGUST 27, 1972 — OLD RENAISSANCE FAIRE GROUNDS, VENETA, OREGON. THE SPRINGFIELD CREAMERY BENEFIT. KEN KESEY'S BROTHER CHUCK AND HIS WIFE SUE WERE GOING UNDER — THEIR YOGURT COMPANY COULDN'T MAKE PAYROLL. AN EMPLOYEE SUGGESTED ASKING THE DEAD. THE BAND WASN'T ENTHUSIASTIC UNTIL THEIR ROADIE RAMROD — WHO'D COME THROUGH THE MERRY PRANKSTERS — SAID: "THESE ARE OUR PEOPLE. WE'RE ALL IN THE SAME FAMILY." THEY ORGANIZED IT IN TWENTY-EIGHT DAYS. TICKETS WERE PRINTED ON YOGURT LABEL INVENTORY — THREE DOLLARS ADVANCE. BETWEEN TWENTY AND FORTY THOUSAND PEOPLE SHOWED UP TO AN OPEN FIELD ON THE LONG TOM RIVER, FIFTEEN MILES WEST OF EUGENE. KEN KESEY AND KEN BABBS EMCEED. IT WAS A HUNDRED AND FIVE DEGREES. THE STAGE FACED WEST — THE SUN WAS IN THE BAND'S FACES ALL AFTERNOON. THEY PLAYED THREE FULL SETS, CONTINUOUSLY UNTIL AFTER DARK. THE DARK STAR AND PLAYING IN THE BAND ARE WIDELY CONSIDERED DEFINITIVE VERSIONS. THIS IS ONE OF THE FIVE GREATEST DEAD SHOWS EVER PLAYED. A FILM WAS SHOT THAT NIGHT BUT SAT UNRELEASED FOR OVER FORTY YEARS — FINALLY REMASTERED AND RELEASED IN 2013. IT INCLUDES FOOTAGE OF THE MERRY PRANKSTERS' 1964 CROSS-COUNTRY BUS TRIP WITH NEAL CASSADY AT THE WHEEL.

SEPTEMBER 21, 1972 — THE SPECTRUM, SOUTH PHILADELPHIA. THE DEAD HADN'T PLAYED HERE IN THREE OR FOUR YEARS — LAST TIME THEY'D BEEN ON A ROUND STAGE IN THE MIDDLE OF THE HALL THAT "SOMEBODY IN THE BAND DIDN'T LIKE." THIS TIME THEY LOVED IT. FIVE HOURS. SEVENTEEN THOUSAND FIVE HUNDRED FANS. ONE CRITIC DESCRIBED GARCIA AND WEIR AS OPERATING SO SEAMLESSLY "THEY SEEM SIAMESE TWINS JOINED AT CONSCIOUSNESS." A FAN: "PLEASE DON'T WAIT SO LONG TO COME BACK." THEY WOULDN'T — THE DEAD PLAYED THE SPECTRUM FIFTY-THREE TIMES, MORE THAN ANY OTHER ACT. BUT THAT'S A 1974 DETAIL. TONIGHT WAS THE REUNION, AND THE BUILDING WITH THE ROOF THAT HAD BLOWN OFF TWICE IN ITS FIRST FIVE MONTHS WAS ROCKING. RELEASED AS DICK'S PICKS VOLUME 36.

NOVEMBER 19, 1972 — HOFHEINZ PAVILION, UNIVERSITY OF HOUSTON. NAMED AFTER JUDGE ROY HOFHEINZ — THE MAN WHO BUILT THE ASTRODOME, SMOKED TWENTY-FIVE SEVEN-INCH CIGARS A DAY, AND "MADE P.T. BARNUM LOOK LIKE FOURTEEN MILES OF BAD ROAD." THE LOW CEILING CREATED UNUSUAL INTIMACY. PARLIAMENT-FUNKADELIC PLAYED SOLD-OUT HALLOWEEN SHOWS HERE. THE DARK STAR HAS "PROBABLY THE BEST" EXTENDED PHIL SOLO OF THE BUNCH. "GORGEOUS SOUND" WITH PHIL HIGH IN THE MIX. SOMEONE NOTICED BOB SINGS "I GRABBED A BOTTLE, CRACKED HIM IN THE JAW" INSTEAD OF THE USUAL LYRIC — "DOES ANYONE KNOW OF OTHER TIMES HE CHANGED IT?" RELEASED FOR RECORD STORE DAY BLACK FRIDAY 2014.

FEBRUARY 9, 1973 — MAPLES PAVILION, STANFORD UNIVERSITY, PALO ALTO. A HOMECOMING IN THE DEEPEST SENSE. GARCIA HAD ARRIVED IN PALO ALTO IN 1960, AGE EIGHTEEN, FRESH FROM AN ARMY DISCHARGE. HE TOOK UP DAILY RESIDENCE AT KEPLER'S BOOKSHOP IN MENLO PARK — "A COLLECTION OF POETS, MUSICIANS, PAINTERS, WRITERS, SOCIALISTS, AND PACIFISTS, WITH A SMATTERING OF OUT AND OUT LUNATICS." HE MET FIFTEEN-YEAR-OLD BOB WEIR AT DANA MORGAN'S MUSIC STORE ON NEW YEAR'S EVE 1963. A CAR CRASH ON JUNIPERO SERRA BOULEVARD IN FEBRUARY 1961 NEARLY KILLED HIM BUT TRANSFORMED HIM: "THAT WAS THE SLINGSHOT FOR THE REST OF MY LIFE." SEVEN SONGS DEBUTED THIS NIGHT — ROW JIMMY, LOOSE LUCY, HERE COMES SUNSHINE, THEY LOVE EACH OTHER, EYES OF THE WORLD, CHINA DOLL, AND WAVE THAT FLAG. SEVEN. IN ONE SHOW. THE WALL OF SOUND PROTOTYPE WAS BEING TESTED — ABOUT TEN SECONDS IN, HALF THE SPEAKERS BLEW. THE BASKETBALL FLOOR UNDULATED WHEN THE CROWD DANCED, CAUSING SPEAKER TOWERS TO SWAY. THE EYES OF THE WORLD DEBUT: "ONE OF THE TOP FIVE VERSIONS, IF NOT THE BEST" — EIGHTEEN MINUTES OF BRAND-NEW MATERIAL PLAYED LIKE IT HAD ALWAYS EXISTED.

FEBRUARY 15, 1973 — DANE COUNTY COLISEUM, MADISON, WISCONSIN. ONE OF THE MOST DOWNLOADED SHOWS ON ARCHIVE.ORG — A HUNDRED PLUS REVIEWS. THE PROMOTER: "I PICKED UP THE DEAD FROM THE AIRPORT. I SAID TO BOB WEIR 'I'VE BEEN TO ABOUT TWENTY-FIVE OF YOUR SHOWS.' OH YEAH, HE SAID, 'I'VE BEEN TO ALL OF THEM.'" THE DARK STAR > EYES OF THE WORLD TRANSITION INCLUDES A PHIL BASS SOLO DESCRIBED AS "THE GIGANTIC ORGASM OF ALL TIME." ONE REVIEWER QUOTED EDGAR CAYCE: "EVERY THOUGHT EVER THOUGHT IN THE HISTORY OF THE UNIVERSE IS RECORDED IN THE UNIVERSAL MIND. I AM LOOKING FORWARD TO FINDING OUT WHAT JERRY WAS THINKING DURING THE HYPER-DRIVE JAM." AN EIGHTEEN-YEAR-OLD FRESHMAN AT HIS FIRST SHOW: "I STILL REMEMBER THE WILD, ECSTATIC LOOK ON A FRIEND'S FACE AS CASEY JONES ENDED THE FIRST SET. THAT IMAGE STICKS IN MY MIND TO THIS DAY." ANOTHER ATTENDEE MET THE WOMAN WHO WOULD BECOME HIS FIRST WIFE AT THE NEXT SHOW DOWN THE ROAD.

FEBRUARY 21, 1973 — ASSEMBLY HALL, CHAMPAIGN. THE FLYING SAUCER BUILT WITH TITAN MISSILE BASE TECHNOLOGY. ABOUT SEVEN THOUSAND FANS. NEW RIDERS OPENED WITH GARCIA ON PEDAL STEEL. THE TRUCKIN' JAM TEASES EYES OF THE WORLD — "LISTENING TO PHIL'S BASS" DURING THE DROP-OUTS REVEALS THE BONES OF WHAT'S COMING. "IN THE WORLD OF GRATEFUL DEAD 1973 THIS IS SOMEWHAT AVERAGE" — WHICH TELLS YOU EVERYTHING ABOUT HOW GOOD 1973 WAS.

FEBRUARY 22, 1973 — ASSEMBLY HALL, CHAMPAIGN. NIGHT THREE. THE PLAYING IN THE BAND "IS ONE OF THE VERY BEST — SUPPLE AND GENTLE, JAZZY YET A LITTLE FREAKY." THE DARK STAR > EYES > CHINA DOLL: "THE MELTDOWN IN THE DARK STAR BRINGS EYES LIKE WINTER SNOW EVENTUALLY BECOMES SPRING RAINS." AN EYEWITNESS: "WHEN THE CONCERT RAN LONGER THAN UNIVERSITY AUTHORITIES THOUGHT IT SHOULD, THEY SHUT DOWN THE STAGE ELECTRICITY AND HALF THE HOUSE LIGHTS. AFTER MUCH BOOING, ONE MORE SONG WAS ALLOWED." ANOTHER: "SET A NEW LAND SPEED RECORD GETTING FROM INDY TO ASSEMBLY HALL." FIFTEEN DAYS BEFORE PIGPEN DIED.

MARCH 24, 1973 — THE SPECTRUM, PHILADELPHIA. SIXTEEN DAYS AFTER PIGPEN DIED. THIRTY-ONE SONGS. THE HE'S GONE WAS ONLY THE SECOND PERFORMANCE SINCE HIS DEATH, AND EVERYONE IN THE ROOM KNEW WHO IT WAS ABOUT NOW. GARCIA HAD SPOKEN AT THE FUNERAL. HE WOULD LATER SAY: "AFTER PIGPEN'S DEATH WE ALL KNEW THIS WAS THE END OF THE ORIGINAL GRATEFUL DEAD." PIGPEN'S HEADSTONE READS: "PIGPEN WAS AND IS NOW FOREVER ONE OF THE GRATEFUL DEAD." A PHOTOGRAPHER HAD TRIED TO BRING THE DYING PIGPEN TO A FINAL BAND REHEARSAL FOR A GROUP PORTRAIT. THE BAND REPORTEDLY REFUSED TO BE PHOTOGRAPHED TOGETHER. HE TOOK PIGPEN'S PORTRAIT ALONE — THE LAST PHOTOGRAPH. THE DARK STAR THAT CLOCKED IN UNDER FIVE MINUTES WAS ONE OF THE SHORTEST AND STRANGEST VERSIONS EVER. RELEASED AS DAVE'S PICKS VOLUME 32.

JUNE 10, 1973 — ROBERT F. KENNEDY MEMORIAL STADIUM, WASHINGTON. EIGHTY THOUSAND PEOPLE ACROSS THE WEEKEND. TICKETS WERE SEVEN DOLLARS. TWO THOUSAND FANS TORE DOWN A GATE FORTY-FIVE MINUTES BEFORE THE SCHEDULED OPENING. DRUG DEALERS ROAMED THE CROWD "SELLING MARIJUANA AND QUAALUDES AS IF THEY WERE BAGS OF PEANUTS." THE DEAD'S ROADIES DOSED THE BACKSTAGE FOOD AND DRINKS WITH LSD. THEIR OWN CREW KNEW TO WATCH OUT, BUT THE ALLMAN BROTHERS' PERSONNEL DIDN'T GET THE WARNING. ONE ABB ROADIE OPENED A CABLE CASE AND STARTED HALLUCINATING. A BRAWL ERUPTED. FOR THE THIRD SET, DICKEY BETTS AND BUTCH TRUCKS OF THE ALLMAN BROTHERS JOINED ALONGSIDE MERL SAUNDERS — THE FIRST TIME THE DEAD HAD PLAYED WITH A SECOND DRUMMER SINCE MICKEY HART'S DEPARTURE IN 1971. KRISHNA CONSCIOUSNESS MEMBERS DISTRIBUTED INCENSE TO INTOXICATED FANS. THE WASHINGTON POST SAID THE ALLMAN BROTHERS "CLEARLY STOLE THE SHOW."

JUNE 22, 1973 — P.N.E. COLISEUM, VANCOUVER. "BEST BIRD SONG EVER? IT MIGHT BE!" THE HE'S GONE > TRUCKIN' IS "ABSOLUTELY EPIC AND MUST BE HEARD." AN EYEWITNESS: "SOME SORT OF DRAPERY OVER THE TOP OF THE WALL OF SOUND TO OBSCURE THE TOP THIRD. THEY PLAYED FOR A LONG TIME." PART OF THE PACIFIC NORTHWEST '73-'74 BOX SET.

JUNE 24, 1973 — PORTLAND MEMORIAL COLISEUM. THE DARK STAR "WILL KNOCK YOUR AUNT CONNIE'S SOCKS OFF." THE EYES THAT FOLLOWS: "SPLENDIFEROUS." PHIL IN PARTICULAR "WORTH LISTENING FOR, HAVING A GREAT SHOW." A RARE SLIPKNOT-LIKE TRANSITION BETWEEN THE DARK STAR AND CHINA DOLL. PART OF THE PACIFIC NORTHWEST '73-'74 BOX SET.

JULY 28, 1973 — WATKINS GLEN RACEWAY, UPSTATE NEW YORK. THE SUMMER JAM. AN ESTIMATED SIX HUNDRED THOUSAND PEOPLE — THE LARGEST CONCERT CROWD IN HISTORY, FIFTY PERCENT MORE THAN WOODSTOCK. ONE IN EVERY THREE HUNDRED AND FIFTY AMERICANS. THE VILLAGE HAD THREE THOUSAND RESIDENTS. THE DAY BEFORE, THE DEAD'S "SOUNDCHECK" HAD TURNED INTO A THREE-HOUR, TWO-SET MARATHON FOR THE HUNDRED THOUSAND PEOPLE ALREADY CAMPED OUT. DURING THE BAND'S SET ON THE ACTUAL DAY, A DRENCHING THUNDERSTORM HIT. EVERYONE ON STAGE FLED EXCEPT GARTH HUDSON OF THE BAND — HE STAYED PLUGGED IN, IMPROVISING ALONE THROUGH THE RAIN, THEN SEAMLESSLY TRANSITIONED INTO "CHEST FEVER" AS THE OTHERS RETURNED. BACKSTAGE: "THE BIGGEST DRUG MARKET ON THE PLANET." A NINETEEN-YEAR-OLD TRADED HOMEMADE WOODEN PIPES FOR "GOOD POT, OPIUM, SPEED, AND LSD." THE DEAD'S SAM CUTLER DEMANDED AN EXTRA TWENTY-FIVE THOUSAND DOLLARS BEFORE THEY'D TAKE THE STAGE. THE ALLMAN BROTHERS' MANAGER NEARLY PULLED HIS BAND WHEN HE FOUND OUT. TWO TEENAGERS — MITCHEL WEISER AND BONNIE BICKWIT — DISAPPEARED HITCHHIKING TO THE FESTIVAL. THE CASE WAS NEVER SOLVED.

NOVEMBER 11, 1973 — WINTERLAND ARENA, SAN FRANCISCO. THE APEX OF THE 1973 JAZZ TRANSFORMATION. WITH PIGPEN GONE AND KEITH GODCHAUX FULLY INTEGRATED, THE BAND HAD MOVED DECISIVELY FROM BLUES-ROCK INTO JAZZ FUSION — WEATHER REPORT, MAHAVISHNU ORCHESTRA, AND HERBIE HANCOCK WERE ALL IN THE AIR. THE SECOND SET WAS A SINGLE CONTINUOUS SUITE: DARK STAR INTO THE MIND LEFT BODY JAM INTO EYES OF THE WORLD INTO CHINA DOLL INTO SUGAR MAGNOLIA — THIRTY-FIVE MINUTES OF CONNECTED MUSIC WHERE EACH TRANSITION WAS A GRADUAL DISSOLUTION, ONE SONG'S JAM THINNING UNTIL ONLY A PULSE REMAINED, THEN A NEW MELODY COALESCING INTO THE NEXT. THIS WAS THE SECOND OF THREE WINTERLAND NIGHTS LATER RELEASED AS THE NINE-CD WINTERLAND 1973: THE COMPLETE RECORDINGS.

NOVEMBER 14, 1973 — SAN DIEGO SPORTS ARENA. BUILT BY BOB BREITBARD, A LOCAL SPORTS VISIONARY WHOSE ARCHITECT USED A TENSION-CABLE SYSTEM TO SUSPEND THE ROOF — NO SUPPORT COLUMNS ANYWHERE. HENDRIX RECORDED HIS LEGENDARY THIRTEEN-MINUTE "RED HOUSE" JAM HERE IN 1969 AND PLAYED HIS FINAL AMERICAN CONCERT HERE IN 1970. THE SECOND SET WAS AN ODYSSEY: THE OTHER ONE KEPT RETURNING LIKE A HOME BASE — TRUCKIN' INTO THE OTHER ONE INTO BIG RIVER INTO THE OTHER ONE INTO EYES OF THE WORLD INTO THE OTHER ONE INTO WHARF RAT. THE BAND USED THE SAME SONG AS A LAUNCHPAD FOR EXCURSIONS AND KEPT CIRCLING BACK. DONNA JEAN WAS VERY PREGNANT. AFTER THE SHOW, WEIR REPORTEDLY GOT INTO A SCUFFLE ON STAGE AND WAS GRABBED BY POLICE. INCLUDED IN THE 30 TRIPS AROUND THE SUN BOX SET.

FEBRUARY 22, 1974 — WINTERLAND ARENA, SAN FRANCISCO. THE FIRST SHOW OF 1974, AND THE FIRST OF A THREE-NIGHT RUN AT THE DEAD'S HOME BASE — A CONVERTED ICE RINK WITH PLYWOOD OVER THE SKATING SURFACE AND A CEILING THAT BOB WEIR CALLED "AN ACOUSTICAL SNAKEPIT." THE BAND HADN'T PLAYED IN TWO MONTHS AND THE WALL OF SOUND WASN'T READY YET — ITS PUBLIC DEBUT WAS STILL A MONTH AWAY. BUT THE EARLY VERSION OF THE SYSTEM WAS IN PLACE, AND DURING A SOUNDCHECK THAT WEEKEND, JACK CASADY OF JEFFERSON AIRPLANE WAS FOUND PLAYING PHIL LESH'S BASS RIG WHILE PHIL SAT ALONE IN THE BACK OF THE EMPTY ARENA, LISTENING — HE WANTED TO HEAR WHAT HIS OWN BASS SOUNDED LIKE THROUGH THE NEW SPEAKERS. THREE SONGS DEBUTED THIS NIGHT: SHIP OF FOOLS, U.S. BLUES, AND IT MUST HAVE BEEN THE ROSES — ALL THREE WOULD APPEAR ON FROM THE MARS HOTEL FOUR MONTHS LATER. THE BAND PLAYED THREE FULL SETS, WITH A THEY LOVE EACH OTHER THAT TURNED OUT TO BE THE LAST-EVER PERFORMANCE OF THE SONG UNTIL IT WAS REVIVED YEARS LATER. SELECTIONS APPEAR AS BONUS TRACKS ON DAVE'S PICKS VOLUME 42.

FEBRUARY 23, 1974 — WINTERLAND ARENA, SAN FRANCISCO. NIGHT TWO. THE LAST HERE COMES SUNSHINE — THE BAND PLAYED IT HERE AND THEN GARCIA APPARENTLY FORGOT THE SONG EXISTED, LATER REFERRING TO IT AS SOMETHING THEY NEVER PLAYED LIVE BECAUSE "IT WASN'T A VERY GOOD SONG." IT WASN'T HEARD AGAIN FOR EIGHTEEN YEARS. FANS NOW RATE THIS VERSION AS THE DEFINITIVE ONE. THE SHOW OPENED WITH AROUND AND AROUND, WHICH WAS NORMALLY A SET CLOSER — AN IMMEDIATE SIGNAL THAT THE BAND CAME TO PLAY. THE SECOND SET CENTERPIECE IS ROUGHLY AN HOUR OF UNBROKEN MUSIC: HE'S GONE DISSOLVING INTO TRUCKIN', THEN DRUMS, THEN A LONG, MOSTLY QUIET OTHER ONE THAT BUILDS SLOWLY WITH EARLY SLIPKNOT RIFFING — THE SKELETON OF A COMPOSITION THAT WOULDN'T FULLY EMERGE UNTIL 1975 — BEFORE OPENING INTO EYES OF THE WORLD. ONE REVIEWER DESCRIBED THE EYES AS "A VAST FIELD OF ELECTRIC FLOWERS AND WARM LIGHT FROM A BRIGHT BLUE SKY." THE NIGHT ENDED WITH THE FULL THREE-SONG FAREWELL: CASEY JONES, JOHNNY B. GOODE, AND WE BID YOU GOODNIGHT. RELEASED AS DAVE'S PICKS VOLUME 42.

FEBRUARY 24, 1974 — WINTERLAND ARENA, SAN FRANCISCO. THE FINAL NIGHT. BILL GRAHAM TOOK THE MICROPHONE AND TOLD THE CROWD: "WHATEVER IS GOING ON WITH THE REST OF THE WORLD, WHETHER IT BE WARS, OR KIDNAPPING, OR CRIMES, THIS IS A PEACEFUL SUNDAY NIGHT WITH THE GRATEFUL DEAD." PATTY HEARST HAD BEEN KIDNAPPED FROM HER BERKELEY APARTMENT TWENTY DAYS EARLIER. NIXON WAS STILL IN THE WHITE HOUSE. THE WAR IN VIETNAM WAS TECHNICALLY OVER BUT NOBODY BELIEVED IT. THE DARK STAR THAT DOMINATES THE SECOND SET RUNS TWENTY-NINE MINUTES. THE BAND DIVES INTO INSTRUMENTAL EXPLORATION IMMEDIATELY AND DOESN'T REACH THE FIRST VERSE UNTIL EIGHTEEN MINUTES IN — A JAZZ-DRIVEN, ANGULAR APPROACH THAT WOULD DEFINE THE REST OF THE YEAR. THE MORNING DEW THAT FOLLOWS — A SONG ABOUT NUCLEAR AFTERMATH — LANDS DIFFERENTLY AFTER GRAHAM'S INTRODUCTION ABOUT PEACE IN A VIOLENT WORLD. ONE CRITIC CALLED IT "DEEP, MOURNFUL, POWERFUL AND RELIGIOUS" WITH "A DEVASTATING MIDDLE SOLO THAT'S TRILLED SO HARD IT MUST HAVE GONE INTO TRIPLE-DIGIT DECIBELS." THE BABY BLUE ENCORE WAS THE FIRST TIME THEY'D PLAYED THE DYLAN SONG SINCE 1972 AND THE LAST TIME UNTIL 1981. AS THE CROWD FILED OUT, PHIL LESH SAID ON MIC: "SEE YA NEXT MONTH AT THE COW PALACE, I HOPE." RELEASED AS DAVE'S PICKS VOLUME 13.

MARCH 23, 1974 — COW PALACE, DALY CITY. THE BUILDING WAS LITERALLY NAMED AFTER COWS — BUILT AS A WPA LIVESTOCK PAVILION DURING THE DEPRESSION, THE NICKNAME CAME FROM A CITY SUPERVISOR WHO CALLED IT "A PALACE FOR COWS." IT HOSTED THE 1964 REPUBLICAN CONVENTION WHERE GOLDWATER DELEGATES BOOED NELSON ROCKEFELLER SO FEROCIOUSLY HE COULDN'T SPEAK. THE BEATLES OPENED THEIR FIRST NORTH AMERICAN TOUR HERE AND TWO THOUSAND FANS RUSHED THE STAGE. TONIGHT WAS BILLED AS "THE SOUND TEST." TICKETS WERE TWO DOLLARS AND FIFTY CENTS. THE DEAD HAD BOUGHT RADIO TIME ON KFRC AND GIVEN AWAY TICKETS, DELIBERATELY DRAWING AN AUDIENCE THAT HAD NEVER SEEN THEM. THE WALL OF SOUND STOOD THREE STORIES HIGH BEHIND THE BAND, HIDDEN BEHIND DARK CURTAINS FOR THE FIRST SET. BETWEEN SETS THE CURTAINS ROSE AND THE FULL SYSTEM WAS REVEALED. SECONDS INTO THE FIRST SONG, THE SYSTEM FAILED. GARCIA PLAYED CHOPIN'S FUNERAL MARCH WHILE THE CREW FIXED IT. WEIR APOLOGIZED: "A THOUSAND PARDONS, FOLKS." ALSO THE LIVE DEBUT OF SCARLET BEGONIAS — WHICH WOULD BE IN THE RECORDING STUDIO ONE WEEK LATER — AND CASSIDY, NAMED FOR BOTH A CREW MEMBER'S INFANT DAUGHTER AND THE DEAD BEAT HERO NEAL CASSADY, WHO'D DIED NEAR A RAILROAD TRACK IN MEXICO SIX YEARS EARLIER. CASSIDY WOULDN'T BE PLAYED AGAIN UNTIL 1976. ONE FAN SPENT THE ENTIRE SHOW SEATED FACING THE REAR WALL AND DIDN'T REALIZE IT UNTIL GRAHAM TURNED ON THE HOUSE LIGHTS — THE SOUND HAD COME FROM EVERYWHERE. RELEASED AS DICK'S PICKS VOLUME 24.

MAY 12, 1974 — MACKAY STADIUM, UNIVERSITY OF NEVADA, RENO. MOTHER'S DAY. AN OUTDOOR FOOTBALL STADIUM AT FORTY-SIX HUNDRED FEET WITH THE SIERRA NEVADA VISIBLE TO THE WEST. THE FIRST WALL OF SOUND SHOW OUTSIDE THE BAY AREA AND THE START OF THE REAL TOUR. THE WASHOE ZEPHYR HIT — A SEASONAL WIND THAT BLOWS DOWN OUT OF THE SIERRA WITH ENOUGH FORCE THAT MARK TWAIN ONCE WROTE IT "ROLLS UP TIN ROOFS LIKE SHEET MUSIC." THE 75-TON SPEAKER ARRAY WAS VISIBLY SWAYING. A GROUP OF FIFTEEN-YEAR-OLDS ON WINDOWPANE ACID THOUGHT THE SPEAKERS WERE BLOWING EVERYONE'S HAIR AND DIDN'T REALIZE IT WAS THE WIND UNTIL THEY STARTED COMING DOWN. THE RENO GAZETTE-JOURNAL COVERED THE EVENT WITH BARELY CONCEALED ALARM — THE CONSERVATIVE CITY DIDN'T KNOW WHAT TO MAKE OF THE HIPPIES OR THEIR ENORMOUS SOUND SYSTEM. SECURITY WANTED TO SEARCH THE CROWD. GARCIA WAS REPORTEDLY UNHAPPY WITH THE PERFORMANCE. AN EYEWITNESS WHO ATTENDED THE SHOW RECALLED HIM SAYING BACKSTAGE THAT THEY PLAYED "SCRAPPY" AND HE WOULDN'T BLAME ANYONE FOR DEMANDING THEIR MONEY BACK. BUT THE TAPE TELLS A DIFFERENT STORY — THE OTHER ONE STRETCHES PAST TWENTY MINUTES WITH THE FIRST MIND LEFT BODY JAM OF 1974, AND THE TRUCKIN' THAT LAUNCHES IT IS WHITE-HOT. INCLUDED IN THE FROM THE MARS HOTEL FIFTIETH ANNIVERSARY DELUXE EDITION AS A COMPLETE PREVIOUSLY UNRELEASED CONCERT.

MAY 14, 1974 — ADAMS FIELD HOUSE, UNIVERSITY OF MONTANA, MISSOULA. THE ONLY GRATEFUL DEAD SHOW EVER PLAYED IN THE STATE OF MONTANA. IT COST TWENTY THOUSAND DOLLARS TO GET THEM THERE. THE CONNECTION WAS ED MCCLANAHAN, A MERRY PRANKSTER AND PLAYBOY CONTRIBUTOR WHO WAS TEACHING AT THE UNIVERSITY — HE'D PUBLISHED "GRATEFUL DEAD I HAVE KNOWN" IN PLAYBOY TWO YEARS EARLIER. SEVEN THOUSAND PEOPLE PACKED A FIELD HOUSE WITH MASSIVE LAMINATED WOOD ARCHES AND A FLOOR THAT WAS ORIGINALLY DIRT. ADMISSION WAS SIX DOLLARS. IT SNOWED. THE CAMPUS AD FOR THE SHOW QUOTED THE BAND'S CONTRACT: "ARTIST'S REPUTATION WILL BE SUBSTANTIALLY AND MATERIALLY DAMAGED IF ARTIST IS PREVENTED FROM PERFORMING FOR SAID FULL FIVE HOURS." THE MISSOULIAN SENT A REVIEWER WHO WROTE THAT GARCIA "TOOK A FORAY INTO ROCK, JAZZ AND FINALLY AN ELECTRONIC NEVERNESS THAT LEFT THE CROWD SPELLBOUND" — BUT CALLED THE DARK STAR "ONE INCOHERENT JAM" THAT "RAMBLED ON FOR FORTY MINUTES." HE ALSO WROTE: "GARCIA IS ONE OF THE BEST MUSICIANS THERE IS." THE SAME DAY WAS THE ANNUAL ABER DAY KEGGER — EIGHT THOUSAND PEOPLE ON A HILLSIDE WITH UNLIMITED BEER. BEFORE THE ENCORE, SOMEONE ARMED WITH A PLASTIC KEGGER PITCHER THREW IT AT THE STAGE AND HIT WEIR IN THE HEAD. WHEN THE BAND CAME BACK, YOU CAN HEAR WEIR SAY: "THANKS A HELL OF A LOT." THE MISSOULIAN EDITORIALIZED: "DRUNK COWBOYS DO NOT BELONG IN A GRATEFUL DEAD CONCERT." RELEASED AS DAVE'S PICKS VOLUME 9.

MAY 17, 1974 — PACIFIC COLISEUM, VANCOUVER. THE ARENA WAS BUILT IN 1968 SPECIFICALLY TO ATTRACT AN NHL FRANCHISE — IT WORKED, AND THE CANUCKS PLAYED THEIR FIRST TWENTY-FIVE SEASONS HERE. IT SITS ON THE GROUNDS OF THE PACIFIC NATIONAL EXHIBITION, VANCOUVER'S ANNUAL SUMMER FAIR, SURROUNDED BY A HORSE TRACK, AN AMUSEMENT PARK, AND A FARMER'S MARKET. THE DEAD HAD PLAYED THREE DIFFERENT BUILDINGS ON THESE GROUNDS SINCE 1966. THIS WAS JUST THE FOURTH SHOW WITH THE COMPLETE WALL OF SOUND, AND THE BAND'S ONLY 1974 VISIT TO CANADA. KEITH GODCHAUX'S FENDER RHODES CAME INTO ITS OWN HERE — REVIEWERS NOTED "AGGRESSIVE, SPOOKY, ATMOSPHERIC" KEYBOARD WORK THAT SURPASSED HIS EARLIER PLAYING. PHIL LESH GENERATED WHAT ONE LISTENER CALLED "TREMENDOUS RESONANCE" THROUGH THE WALL'S DEDICATED BASS COLUMNS. LESH WOULD LATER DESCRIBE THE EXPERIENCE OF PLAYING THROUGH THE SYSTEM AS "PILOTING A FLYING SAUCER. OR RIDING YOUR OWN SOUND WAVE." MONEY MONEY MADE ITS FIRST APPEARANCE — ONE OF ONLY THREE PERFORMANCES BEFORE THE SONG WAS PERMANENTLY BURIED. THE VERSION HERE IS THE ONE USED ON THE FROM THE MARS HOTEL ALBUM. PART OF THE PACIFIC NORTHWEST '73-'74 BOX SET — A NINETEEN-CD SET IN A FIRST NATIONS ART BOX BY ROY HENRY VICKERS, NOMINATED FOR A GRAMMY.

MAY 19, 1974 — PORTLAND MEMORIAL COLISEUM. THE BUILDING STANDS ON JUST FOUR CONCRETE PIERS DESPITE BEING DOUBLE THE SIZE OF A CITY BLOCK. EIGHTY THOUSAND SQUARE FEET OF GLASS WINDOWS WRAP THE EXTERIOR — WHEN THE BLACKOUT CURTAINS ARE OPEN, IT MAY BE THE ONLY ARENA IN THE WORLD WITH A THREE-HUNDRED-AND-SIXTY-DEGREE VIEW TO THE OUTSIDE. THE BEATLES PLAYED HERE IN 1965 AND ALLEN GINSBERG ATTENDED BOTH SHOWS AND WROTE A POEM ABOUT THE CROWD. THE TRAIL BLAZERS WON A CHAMPIONSHIP ON THIS FLOOR. THE OREGONIAN NOTED "THOUSANDS OF FANS BRAVING THE PORTLAND DRIZZLE" TO GET IN. THE BAND TOOK THE STAGE FIFTEEN MINUTES EARLY. DAVID LEMIEUX, THE GRATEFUL DEAD'S ARCHIVIST, LATER PLACED THIS SHOW "AMONGST THE BEST DEAD SHOW EVER, UP THERE WITH HARPUR COLLEGE, VENETA, AND CORNELL." DAVID CROSBY SAID ON KPFA THAT IF HE WERE STRANDED ON A DESERT ISLAND, THIS IS THE TAPE HE'D BRING. THE SECOND SET IS WHY. THE JAM COMING OUT OF TRUCKIN' IS CONSIDERED BY MANY TO BE THE FINEST THE BAND EVER PLAYED — GARCIA ON A DISTORTED TONE, WEIR PLAYING STACCATO CHORDS, THE WHOLE THING BUILDING INTO A MIND LEFT BODY JAM WHERE LESH WANTED TO START NOT FADE AWAY BUT WEIR HAD OTHER IDEAS. THE SBD DIDN'T CIRCULATE UNTIL 1998. PART OF THE PACIFIC NORTHWEST '73-'74 BOX SET.

MAY 21, 1974 — HEC EDMUNDSON PAVILION, UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON, SEATTLE. BUILT IN NINE MONTHS IN 1927 FOR SIX HUNDRED THOUSAND DOLLARS RAISED ALMOST ENTIRELY BY STUDENTS. WHEN IT OPENED IT WAS THE LARGEST ARENA WEST OF THE MISSISSIPPI. NAMED AFTER THE BASKETBALL COACH WHO COMPETED IN THE 1912 STOCKHOLM OLYMPICS AND INVENTED THE FAST BREAK. MASSIVE LAMINATED ARCHES AND FORTY THOUSAND SQUARE FEET OF SKYLIGHTS. THE ENTRY WAS CASUAL — NO MAZES, NO HEAVY SECURITY, PARKING TWO HUNDRED FEET FROM THE DOOR. KAREN JACKSON FORBES, A UW ALUMNA, PLANNED TO LEAVE AT INTERMISSION. SHE STAYED FIVE HOURS AND WENT ON TO ATTEND EVERY DEAD CONCERT SHE COULD: "IT WAS ABSOLUTELY JUST YOU AND THE BAND. HEC ED WAS A REALLY GREAT PLACE TO HAVE IT." THE SEATTLE TIMES WROTE: "EVERYBODY IN THE HALL LAST NIGHT LITERALLY FELT THE MUSIC." THIS WAS THE LONGEST SINGLE SONG THE GRATEFUL DEAD EVER PLAYED: FORTY-SIX MINUTES AND FIFTY-FIVE SECONDS OF PLAYING IN THE BAND, OPENING THE SECOND SET. DAVID LEMIEUX: "WHEN THE DEAD TOOK THOSE CHANCES AND PLAYED A FORTY-SEVEN-MINUTE VERSION IT INDICATES THEY WERE IN A PRETTY 'ON' SPACE. THEY WOULDN'T HAVE ATTEMPTED SOMETHING LIKE THAT IF THEY WEREN'T FEELING IT." IT WAS ONLY THE FIFTH SHOW OF THE TOURING WALL OF SOUND. ANOTHER ATTENDEE, RANDY BOWLES, LEFT BEFORE THE SHOW ENDED — NOT FROM DISAPPOINTMENT BUT FROM EMOTIONAL SATURATION. PART OF THE PACIFIC NORTHWEST '73-'74 BOX SET.

JUNE 8, 1974 — OAKLAND COLISEUM STADIUM. THE FIRST BILL GRAHAM "DAY ON THE GREEN" — A FORMAT THAT WOULD DEFINE BAY AREA STADIUM CONCERTS FOR THE NEXT TWO DECADES. GRAHAM WAS A GERMAN-BORN JEWISH REFUGEE WHO'D FLED BERLIN AFTER KRISTALLNACHT AND ARRIVED IN NEW YORK VIA THE RED CROSS AT AGE SIX. HE VIEWED THESE SHOWS AS "CULTURAL EVENTS, NOT MERE TRANSACTIONS." GATES OPENED AT TEN. COMMANDER CODY PLAYED FIRST AND ASKED THE CROWD: "HOW COME I DON'T SEE MORE OF YOU HIPPIES AT BASEBALL GAMES?" — THE A'S WERE THE REIGNING WORLD CHAMPIONS AND THIS WAS THEIR STADIUM. ABOUT THIRTY THOUSAND PEOPLE SPREAD ACROSS THE OUTFIELD GRASS WITH BLANKETS AND PICNICS IN A VENUE THAT HELD SIXTY THOUSAND. IT WAS PASTORAL, UNHURRIED, THE OPPOSITE OF NIGHTTIME ARENA INTENSITY. THE BEACH BOYS WERE LATE — STUCK IN TRAFFIC FROM SAN FRANCISCO — AND THEIR CREW REFUSED TO USE THE WALL OF SOUND, INSISTING ON THEIR OWN PA. OWSLEY STANLEY WAS SO UPSET HE BROKE HIS ARM FALLING FROM THE SCAFFOLDING WHILE TAKING DOWN THEIR EQUIPMENT. THE DEAD VOLUNTARILY SHORTENED THEIR SET TO ACCOMMODATE THE DELAY. THAT EVENING, GARCIA DROVE TO THE GREAT AMERICAN MUSIC HALL AND PLAYED ANOTHER FULL SHOW WITH MERL SAUNDERS. ONE FAN DESCRIBED A WAVE OF CLAPPING TRAVELING ACROSS THE STADIUM AT THE SPEED OF SOUND — "PRETTY INTENSE IN AN ALTERED STATE." THE AUD TAPES ARE SO GOOD THEY REGULARLY CIRCULATE MISLABELED AS SOUNDBOARD.

JUNE 16, 1974 — IOWA STATE FAIRGROUNDS, DES MOINES. THE STAGE SAT IN THE CENTER OF THE RACETRACK WITH THE WALL OF SOUND AIMED AT THE GRANDSTAND — A 600-FOOT CURVED BRICK STRUCTURE BUILT IN 1927 AS A MEMORIAL TO IOWANS WHO DIED IN THE FIRST WORLD WAR. PHIL LESH TOLD THE CROWD: "WE'RE NOT GOING TO PLAY ALL NIGHT, BUT WE ARE GOING TO PLAY ALL DAY." THEY WENT FROM 12:30 TO ABOUT 5:30 IN THE AFTERNOON SUN. ONE DETAIL THAT DOESN'T SURVIVE ON TAPE: PHIL'S BASS THROUGH THE WALL OF SOUND COULD BE STEERED SPATIALLY — AN ATTENDEE DESCRIBED THE SENSATION OF THE LOW END MOVING FROM SIDE TO SIDE, UP AND DOWN, DURING THE LONG JAMS AS "PISS-YOUR-PANTS EXCITING." BILL GRAHAM WAS THERE, KNEELING AT THE FRONT OF THE STAGE. BEHIND THE STAGE, ELABORATE TENTS SERVED WHAT ONE FAN CALLED "APPARENTLY AMAZING DELICACIES." THE EQUIPMENT METERS GLOWED WITH LITTLE GREEN LIGHTS "PRODUCING ENERGY IN SOMETHING OTHER THAN THE NORMAL VISUAL SPECTRUM." THREE FULL SETS ON A BEAUTIFUL, SUNNY, NOT-TOO-HOT IOWA AFTERNOON. PHIL HAD A BEARD; JERRY DIDN'T. RELEASED ON ROAD TRIPS VOLUME 2 NUMBER 3.

JUNE 18, 1974 — FREEDOM HALL, LOUISVILLE. BUILT IN 1956 FOR THE WORLD'S CHAMPIONSHIP HORSE SHOW — THE ARENA FLOOR WAS DESIGNED TO ACCOMMODATE A THREE-HUNDRED-FOOT SHOW RING WHERE SADDLEBREDS PARADED IN FIVE GAITS. TONIGHT IT HELD THE GRATEFUL DEAD. IT WAS BILLED AS "FOUR HOURS WITH THE GRATEFUL DEAD." YOU COULD WALK UP AND BUY TICKETS. DURING BEAT IT ON DOWN THE LINE, GARCIA DROPPED A FEW BARS OF "MY OLD KENTUCKY HOME" — STEPHEN FOSTER'S SONG, PLAYED BEFORE EVERY KENTUCKY DERBY SINCE 1921. SOMEBODY THREW A BOTTLE TOWARD THE STAGE. WEIR ASKED THE CROWD TO PASS BOTTLES FORWARD RATHER THAN THROW THEM. PHIL LESH ADDED: "YEAH, WE COULD USE THE THREE CENTS." THEN A WOMAN SCREAMED — AND LESH DROPPED THE ATOM-BOMB OPENING NOTE OF THE OTHER ONE. WHAT FOLLOWED WAS THE ONLY "IT'S A SIN" JAM IN ALL OF 1974, LAST HEARD IN AUGUST 1969. LOUISVILLE WAS A BORDER CITY — BOURBON AND HORSE RACING TO ITS BONES, SITTING ON THE OHIO RIVER ACROSS FROM INDIANA. MUHAMMAD ALI WAS BORN HERE. THE MORNING DEW ENCORE WAS ONE OF ONLY THREE TIMES THE DEAD EVER CLOSED A SHOW WITH THAT SONG. RELEASED ON ROAD TRIPS VOLUME 2 NUMBER 3.

JUNE 20, 1974 — ATLANTA. THE VENUE IS DISPUTED — DEADBASE SAYS THE MUNICIPAL AUDITORIUM; A TICKET STUB CONFIRMS THE OMNI. BOTH WERE IN ATLANTA, AND BOTH WERE THE KIND OF PLACE WHERE THINGS WENT WRONG: THE OMNI WAS CLAD IN COR-TEN STEEL DESIGNED TO RUST TO A PROTECTIVE PATINA, BUT IN ATLANTA'S HUMIDITY IT JUST KEPT CORRODING UNTIL YOU COULD CLIMB THROUGH THE HOLES IN THE WALLS. MARIA MULDAUR OPENED WITH "MIDNIGHT AT THE OASIS." DURING THE BREAK, THE AMPS BLEW UP — SMOKE AND SPARKS ON STAGE LEFT. AN ATTENDEE SLIPPED PAST BIKER SECURITY AND FOUND GARCIA SITTING ALONE ON A WOODEN SAWHORSE, SMOKING A CIGARETTE. JERRY HANDED THEM PASSES TO THE AFTER-PARTY: "IT'S COOL, MAN, THEY'RE WITH ME." SLIPKNOT! WAS BORN AT THIS SHOW — THE MUSICAL IDEA THAT WOULD BECOME THE CENTER OF HELP ON THE WAY > SLIPKNOT! > FRANKLIN'S TOWER. IT EMERGED ORGANICALLY FROM AN EYES OF THE WORLD JAM AND YOU CAN HEAR IT TAKING SHAPE IN REAL TIME. WHEN THE BAND FINISHED, PHIL LESH SAID "THANK YOU AND GOODNIGHT" WITH SUCH FINALITY THAT NO ONE ASKED FOR AN ENCORE. THEY LIT THE DISCO BALL FOR SUGAR MAGNOLIA.

JUNE 22, 1974 — JAI-ALAI FRONTON, MIAMI. JAI ALAI IS THE FASTEST BALL SPORT IN THE WORLD — PLAYERS HURL A HARD RUBBER BALL AT A GRANITE WALL AT A HUNDRED AND FIFTY MILES PER HOUR WHILE GAMBLERS BET IN TIERED SEATS BEHIND A SCREEN. IN 1974, SOUTH FLORIDA HAD NO MAJOR SPORTS ARENAS, SO THE FRONTONS — PURPOSE-BUILT GAMBLING FACILITIES FOR A BASQUE SPORT — DOUBLED AS CONCERT HALLS. SINATRA, HENDRIX, SPRINGSTEEN, AND BOB MARLEY ALL PLAYED FRONTONS. IT WAS SIMPLY WHERE YOU PLAYED IF YOU WERE A MAJOR ACT COMING THROUGH MIAMI. THE SOUNDCHECK WAS CAPTURED ON TAPE AND IT'S WORTH HEARING: THE BAND WANDERS THROUGH A NOBODY'S JAM INTO THE OTHER ONE INTO A TIGER THAT STRETCHES FOR TWENTY-FIVE MINUTES. THIRD ROW SEATS WERE AVAILABLE FOR TWENTY DOLLARS. MIAMI IN JUNE OF 1974 WAS SUN, HUMIDITY, ART DECO DECAY, RETIREES, CUBAN EXILES, AND THE FRONTON — PABLO ESCOBAR AND THE COCAINE COWBOYS WERE STILL A FEW YEARS AWAY. NEARLY EIGHTY MINUTES FROM THIS NIGHT APPEAR AS THE BONUS DISC ON DAVE'S PICKS VOLUME 34.

JUNE 23, 1974 — JAI-ALAI FRONTON, MIAMI. NIGHT TWO. ONE FAN FLEW IN ON A CARGO PLANE FROM PUERTO RICO THROUGH THE BERMUDA TRIANGLE — THEY STRAPPED INTO SURPLUS GREYHOUND BUS SEATS BOLTED TO THE DECK BEHIND TEN TONS OF TEXTILES. HE HAD ABOUT THIRTY DOLLARS. BEHIND THE WALL OF SOUND WAS A WALL OF SOLID GRANITE — THE JAI-ALAI PLAYING SURFACE. THE ACOUSTIC PROPERTIES WERE UNIQUE AND UNREPEATABLE. TWO THINGS HAPPENED HERE THAT NEVER HAPPENED ANYWHERE ELSE: THE DEAD PLAYED CHUCK BERRY'S "LET IT ROCK" — ONCE, AND THEN NEVER AGAIN. AND NED LAGIN AND PHIL LESH PERFORMED SEASTONES LIVE FOR THE FIRST TIME — EXPERIMENTAL ELECTRONIC MUSIC THAT WOULD BECOME A REGULAR INTERMISSION FEATURE FOR THE REST OF THE YEAR. AT ONE POINT, AN AUDIENCE MEMBER YELLED "ALLIGATOR!" BEFORE THE DARK STAR, APPARENTLY UNAWARE THAT PIGPEN — THE ONLY PERSON WHO'D EVER SUNG IT — HAD BEEN DEAD FOR OVER A YEAR. RELEASED AS DAVE'S PICKS VOLUME 34.

JUNE 26, 1974 — PROVIDENCE CIVIC CENTER. ONE REVIEWER CALLED IT "A HORRID VENUE IN ALMOST ALL WAYS." PROVIDENCE IN 1974 WAS A CITY IN DEEP DECLINE — POPULATION FALLING FASTER THAN ANYWHERE EXCEPT DETROIT, DEPARTMENT STORES CLOSED, BUILDINGS BOARDED UP. RAYMOND PATRIARCA, BOSS OF THE NEW ENGLAND MAFIA, RAN HIS EMPIRE FROM FEDERAL HILL A FEW MILES AWAY. THE GLEAMING NEW CIVIC CENTER, OPENED JUST TWO YEARS EARLIER, WAS ONE OF THE FEW BRIGHT SPOTS IN A DARK ERA. THE DEAD PLAYED A MARATHON WITH THREE SETS AND A SEASTONES INTERMISSION. A SPEAKER BLEW DURING SCARLET BEGONIAS, ADDING A SLIGHT METALLIC REVERB TO THE VOCALS. THE THIRD SET OPENED WITH A BEER BARREL POLKA TEASE BEFORE TRUCKIN' LAUNCHED INTO A SEQUENCE THAT DEADBASE TIMES AT THIRTY-ONE MINUTES AND THIRTY-SEVEN SECONDS — ALL OF IT CATALOGUED UNDER A SINGLE ENTRY BECAUSE THE SONGS WERE INSEPARABLE. IT INCLUDED THE OTHER ONE, A SPANISH JAM, AND A WHARF RAT THAT THE AUDIENCE DIDN'T WANT TO END. RELEASED AS DICK'S PICKS VOLUME 12.

JUNE 28, 1974 — BOSTON GARDEN. AN OLD BARN ABOVE NORTH STATION DESIGNED BY A BOXING PROMOTER WHO WANTED EVERY SEAT CLOSE ENOUGH TO SEE THE SWEAT ON A FIGHTER'S BROW. THERE WAS NO AIR CONDITIONING. RATS LIVED IN THE BOWELS OF THE BUILDING; AT ONE POINT, MONKEYS THAT ESCAPED FROM A CIRCUS TOOK UP RESIDENCE IN THE RAFTERS. THE PARQUET BASKETBALL FLOOR HAD DEAD SPOTS WHERE THE BALL REFUSED TO BOUNCE. DURING STANLEY CUP PLAYOFFS THE ICE PRODUCED FOG SO THICK THEY HAD TO STOP THE GAME. FROM THE MARS HOTEL HAD BEEN RELEASED THE DAY BEFORE. THE SECOND SET'S WEATHER REPORT SUITE SPAWNED A THIRTY-MINUTE JAM DESCRIBED AS "HINTING AT ALL SORTS OF SONGS, INCLUDING DARK STAR, ALONG THE WAY" — A SINGLE EXTENDED CREATION THAT EXISTS NOWHERE ELSE IN THE CATALOG. THIS WAS THE DEAD'S FIRST SHOW AT BOSTON GARDEN. THEY WOULD PLAY IT TWENTY-TWO MORE TIMES BEFORE THE BUILDING WAS DEMOLISHED IN 1998 AND REPLACED BY A PARKING LOT. RELEASED AS DICK'S PICKS VOLUME 12.

JUNE 30, 1974 — SPRINGFIELD CIVIC CENTER. THE LAST NIGHT OF A LONG, PUNISHING TOUR. ONE REVIEWER CALLED IT "THE VALIUM SHOW OF JUNE 1974" — JERRY SEEMED SUBDUED, THERE WERE GIGGLES AND LAUGHS MID-SONG AND MISSED LYRICS. THE BAND WAS CLEARLY READY TO STOP. BUT SPRINGFIELD IS THE BIRTHPLACE OF BASKETBALL — JAMES NAISMITH INVENTED THE GAME HERE IN 1891 — AND SOMETHING ABOUT THE ROOM KEPT THEM PLAYING. THE FIRST SET CLOSED WITH A PLAYING IN THE BAND > UNCLE JOHN'S BAND > PLAYING IN THE BAND SANDWICH — AN ARCHITECTURAL MOVE USUALLY RESERVED FOR THE SECOND SET. THE EYES OF THE WORLD AND TRUCKIN' JAMS IN THE SECOND SET STILL HAD PLENTY OF HEAT DESPITE THE FATIGUE. FOUR YEARS LATER, THE CIVIC CENTER'S SISTER BUILDING IN HARTFORD WOULD SUFFER ONE OF THE MOST DRAMATIC STRUCTURAL FAILURES IN AMERICAN SPORTS HISTORY — FOURTEEN HUNDRED TONS OF STEEL CRASHING DOWN AT 4:19 AM, SIX HOURS AFTER FIVE THOUSAND BASKETBALL FANS HAD BEEN SITTING UNDERNEATH IT. NOBODY WAS HURT.

JULY 19, 1974 — SELLAND ARENA, FRESNO. KEITH GODCHAUX'S BIRTHDAY. THE DEAD IN FRESNO MEANT THE WALL OF SOUND IN THE SAN JOAQUIN VALLEY — THE MOST PRODUCTIVE AGRICULTURAL REGION IN THE WORLD, FLAT AND HOT AND DRY, WHERE COTTON PICKERS WORKED IN HUNDRED-DEGREE HEAT ON THE WESTSIDE. FRESNO WAS NOT GLAMOROUS. BUT IT SAT MIDWAY BETWEEN SAN FRANCISCO AND LOS ANGELES AND A LOT OF GREAT BANDS PLAYED HERE ON SCHOOL NIGHTS. THE CROWD WAS THIN — THE 6,500-SEAT ARENA WAS NOT FULL. IT HAD BEEN THREE WEEKS SINCE THE LAST SHOW AND THERE WERE MINOR LYRIC MISCUES. BUT THE THIRD SET IS SOMETHING ELSE: WEATHER REPORT SUITE INTO SPANISH JAM INTO A JAM INTO EYES OF THE WORLD INTO CHINA DOLL — OVER AN HOUR OF CONTINUOUS MUSIC. ONE REVIEWER CALLED THE SPANISH JAM "SO HEAVY, SO THICK, QUITE SCARY FOR THE UNINITIATED." PHIL WAS "A BEAST, DROPPIN' BOMBS LIKE A MADMAN." A SEVENTEEN-YEAR-OLD WHO CAME ON A COUSIN'S SUGGESTION REALIZED DURING U.S. BLUES THAT "THIS WAS WHERE IT WAS AT." RELEASED AS DAVE'S PICKS VOLUME 17.

JULY 25, 1974 — INTERNATIONAL AMPHITHEATRE, CHICAGO. BUILT IN SEVEN MONTHS IN 1934 AFTER A FIRE DESTROYED THE HORSE AUCTION BARN AT THE UNION STOCK YARDS — THE LEGENDARY SLAUGHTERHOUSE DISTRICT THAT INSPIRED UPTON SINCLAIR'S "THE JUNGLE." THE STOCKYARDS HAD CLOSED THREE YEARS EARLIER BUT THE SMELL OF MANURE AND ANIMAL PROCESSING STILL HUNG OVER THE NEIGHBORHOOD. THIS WAS ALSO THE BUILDING WHERE THE 1968 DEMOCRATIC CONVENTION TOOK PLACE — WHERE TEN THOUSAND DEMONSTRATORS MET TWENTY-THREE THOUSAND POLICE AND NATIONAL GUARDSMEN WHILE THE WHOLE WORLD WATCHED. MAYOR DALEY HAD THE MAIN DOORS BULLETPROOFED AND THE BUILDING SURROUNDED BY BARBED WIRE. SIX YEARS LATER, THE DEAD BROUGHT THEIR OWN MASSIVE STRUCTURE — THE WALL OF SOUND — INTO THIS SAME ROOM. IT WAS THE ONLY DEAD SHOW EVER PLAYED HERE. THE RECORDING CIRCULATES IN WHAT DEADBASE DESCRIBES AS "A HORRIBLE QUALITY AUD" — ONE OF THE WORST TAPES OF THE YEAR. BUT THE DARK STAR CONTAINS A SLIPKNOT! TEASE AT THE TWENTY-ONE-MINUTE MARK THAT ONE REVIEWER CALLED "THE LAST REALLY INCREDIBLE STAR OF THE SEVENTIES." DEMOLISHED 1999.

JULY 27, 1974 — ROANOKE CIVIC CENTER. A SMALL RAILROAD CITY IN THE BLUE RIDGE MOUNTAINS. ROANOKE HAD BOOMED IN THE 1880S WHEN THE NORFOLK AND WESTERN RAILWAY HEADQUARTERED THERE, BUT BY 1974 DIESEL HAD REPLACED STEAM, THE VISCOSE PLANT HAD CLOSED, AND THE GIANT ILLUMINATED STAR ON MILL MOUNTAIN — VISIBLE FOR MILES — SHONE OVER A CITY SEARCHING FOR A NEW IDENTITY. DONNA JEAN WAS THERE BUT ILL — A STOMACH PROBLEM KEPT HER FROM SINGING ON MOST SONGS. THE CROWD WAS HOSTILE, YELLING BETWEEN NUMBERS, AND THE BAND SEEMED WARY. AN EYEWITNESS WHO ATTENDED SAYS THERE WAS NO EYES OF THE WORLD ENCORE DESPITE DEADBASE LISTING ONE — AN ERROR "PERPETUATED THROUGH THE YEARS." AFTER THE LAST SONG, THE CREW'S RAMROD SHOOED THE REMAINING AUDIENCE OUT WHILE THE WALL OF SOUND WAS DISMANTLED AROUND THEM. BUT DURING U.S. BLUES, JERRY DECIDED HE'D RATHER KEEP PLAYING — AND THE BAND FELL INTO A FIVE-MINUTE JAM ON THE MELODY THAT WAS NEVER ATTEMPTED BEFORE AND NEVER REPEATED. ONE ATTENDEE DROVE HOME ALONG SKYLINE DRIVE TO WASHINGTON IN THE DARK.

JULY 29, 1974 — CAPITAL CENTRE, LANDOVER, MARYLAND. BRAND NEW — IT HAD OPENED EIGHT MONTHS EARLIER AND STILL SMELLED LIKE FRESH CONCRETE. IT WAS THE FIRST ARENA IN AMERICA WITH A VIDEO REPLAY SCREEN, THE FIRST WITH LUXURY SKY SUITES, THE FIRST WITH COMPUTERIZED TICKETING. THE PARKING LOT WAS DIVIDED INTO PATRIOTICALLY THEMED SECTIONS — LIBERTY BELL, CAPITOL, EAGLE, STARS AND STRIPES — SO YOU COULD FIND YOUR CAR. ONE REGULAR DESCRIBED IT AS "BY FAR THE DARKEST ARENA I HAVE EVER BEEN IN." THIS WAS THE DEAD'S FIRST OF WHAT WOULD BECOME TWENTY-NINE SHOWS HERE. THE SECOND SET'S CONNECTED JAM SUITE — HE'S GONE INTO TRUCKIN' INTO THE OTHER ONE INTO SPANISH JAM INTO WHARF RAT — DREW RAVES. A REVIEWER WRITING DURING HURRICANE SANDY IN 2012 SAID THE WEATHER REPORT SUITE FROM THIS SHOW WAS THE FIRST THING THAT CAME TO MIND AFTER THE STORM. TWELVE YEARS LATER, THE PARKING LOT WAS IMMORTALIZED IN THE CULT DOCUMENTARY "HEAVY METAL PARKING LOT," FILMED BEFORE A JUDAS PRIEST CONCERT. SELECTIONS APPEARED ON THE 2012 DAVE'S PICKS BONUS DISC.

JULY 31, 1974 — DILLON STADIUM, HARTFORD. A WPA FOOTBALL STADIUM BUILT IN 1935 ON LAND DONATED BY THE COLT FIREARMS FAMILY — OPEN AIR, BLEACHERS, AND INTERSTATE 91 CLOSE ENOUGH THAT PASSING TRUCKS SOUND LIKE AIRPLANES ON THE TAPE. THE BAND WAS BACKSTAGE "LIT UP WITH LAUGHTER" BEFORE THE SHOW — EVERYTHING WAS WORKING ON THIS SUNNY AFTERNOON. THE TAPER, BILLY DEGEN, HAD EATEN MUSHROOMS AND INITIALLY COULDN'T SET UP HIS EQUIPMENT, STRUGGLING WITH "DIODES, PLASTIC COATINGS, AND VIBRATORY ELECTRICAL FIELDS." BUT HE GOT IT TOGETHER AND CAPTURED ONE OF THE BEST DOCUMENTS OF THE WALL OF SOUND OUTDOORS — WHAT THE SYSTEM REALLY SOUNDED LIKE ACROSS AN OPEN STADIUM ON A SUMMER NIGHT. DURING ROW JIMMY, THE SUN SET DIRECTLY BEHIND BOB WEIR'S HEAD. WHEN TRUCKIN' HIT, THEY TURNED ON ALL THE STADIUM LIGHTS AND THE CROWD JUST LIT UP. A FIFTEEN-YEAR-OLD ON WINDOWPANE ACID WAS IN THE STANDS. A FOURTEEN-YEAR-OLD WAS "STUNNED AT HOW LONG AND GRACEFUL IT WAS." A FIRST-TIMER WHO'D TAKEN THE TRAIN FROM NEW JERSEY ON A SNAP DECISION CALLED IT "THE BEST SNAP DECISION I EVER MADE." PENULTIMATE OUTDOOR WALL OF SOUND SHOW. RELEASED AS DAVE'S PICKS VOLUME 2.

AUGUST 4, 1974 — PHILADELPHIA CIVIC CENTER. AN ART DECO LANDMARK BUILT IN 1931 — LIMESTONE FRIEZES DEPICTING INDUSTRIAL THEMES, GEOMETRIC SETBACKS, AN 86-RANK PIPE ORGAN. IT HOSTED FOUR PRESIDENTIAL NOMINATING CONVENTIONS, INCLUDING THE SWELTERING 1948 TRIPLE HEADER WHERE TRUMAN, DEWEY, AND HENRY WALLACE WERE ALL NOMINATED IN THE SAME BUILDING IN THE SAME SUMMER WITH NO AIR CONDITIONING. MARTIN LUTHER KING JR. SPOKE HERE. THE JACKSON 5 PLAYED THEIR FIRST OFFICIAL MOTOWN CONCERT HERE. THE DEAD WERE AT THE CIVIC CENTER INSTEAD OF THE LARGER SPECTRUM BECAUSE A ROAD CREW MEMBER HAD DUMPED SPAGHETTI ON THE PROMOTER'S HEAD THE YEAR BEFORE. TWELVE THOUSAND GENERAL ADMISSION — A "DANCE CONCERT." BEFORE THE SHOW: "EVERYWHERE YOU LOOK THERE IS THAT GENTLE SMILE AND GLEAM IN THE EYE THAT ALWAYS PRECEDES A DEAD CONCERT." DURING THE BREAK, PHIL LESH CAME OUT ALONE WITH HIS SYNTHESIZER BASS AND PLAYED FOR TWENTY MINUTES — THE AUDIENCE "STANDING ROCKING BACK AND FORTH ON THEIR HEELS, OR SITTING IN THE LOTUS POSITION, EYES CLOSED AND BODIES SLOWLY SWAYING." TOTAL STAGE TIME: FIVE AND A HALF HOURS. FOUR DAYS LATER, NIXON RESIGNED. RELEASED AS PART OF DICK'S PICKS VOLUME 31.

AUGUST 5, 1974 — PHILADELPHIA CIVIC CENTER. NIGHT TWO. GARCIA TOOK THE MICROPHONE AND TOLD THE CROWD: "YOU PEOPLE ARE TOO MUCH! THANKS FOR EVERYTHING." THE THIRTY-ONE-MINUTE TRUCKIN' IN THE THIRD SET WAS DESCRIBED AS "INSANE" — THE SCARLET BEGONIAS AT ELEVEN MINUTES WAS BETTER THAN THE PREVIOUS NIGHT'S. AFTER FIVE AND A HALF HOURS ACROSS TWO NIGHTS, THE ROAD CREW — "STILL UP FROM THESE TWO FABULOUS NIGHTS" AND FUELED BY WHAT A JOURNALIST PRESENT EUPHEMISTICALLY DESCRIBED AS "A GOOD DOSE OF COCAINE" — BROKE DOWN THE WALL OF SOUND OVERNIGHT AND SHIPPED IT TO JERSEY CITY FOR THE NEXT SHOW. THE BUILDING WAS DEMOLISHED IN 2005 AFTER ASBESTOS CONTAMINATION MADE PRESERVATION IMPOSSIBLE. THE REPLACEMENT COST THREE HUNDRED AND TWO MILLION DOLLARS. RELEASED AS PART OF DICK'S PICKS VOLUME 31.

AUGUST 6, 1974 — ROOSEVELT STADIUM, JERSEY CITY. THIS SHOW WAS SUPPOSED TO HAPPEN FOUR DAYS EARLIER. ON AUGUST 2ND, THE SKY WENT FROM BRIGHT BLUE TO "DEEP, DARK IMPENDING BLACK" AND IT RAINED FOR TWO HOURS. GARCIA AND WEIR GOT ELECTRICAL SHOCKS ON THE STAGE. WEIR CAME OUT TO EXPLAIN THE DANGER; SOMEONE THREW A BOTTLE AT HIS HEAD. HELL'S ANGELS BEAT THE THROWER. THEY HANDED OUT EXPIRED IKE AND TINA TURNER TICKETS AS RAIN CHECKS. FANS SOAKED THEIR OWN STUBS IN WATER UNTIL THEY WERE UNREADABLE AND CLAIMED EXTRAS. THE RESCHEDULED SHOW MAY HAVE HAD DOUBLE THE NORMAL CAPACITY. JACKIE ROBINSON HAD BROKEN PROFESSIONAL BASEBALL'S COLOR LINE AT THIS STADIUM IN 1946 — GOING FOUR FOR FIVE WITH A HOME RUN. BY 1974 IT WAS A CRUMBLING WPA RELIC IN A DECAYING CITY WITH A ROOFLESS STAGE AND AN EIGHT-FOOT BARBED-WIRE FENCE SEPARATING THE CROWD FROM THE BAND. THE WATERGATE "SMOKING GUN" TAPE HAD BEEN RELEASED THE DAY BEFORE. BOBBY TOLD SOMEONE CLIMBING THE FENCE: "DON'T CLIMB ON THE FENCE, IDIOT" — YOU CAN HEAR IT ON THE TAPE. AT MIDNIGHT, FIREWORKS SPELLED "THE GRATEFUL DEAD" IN RED, WHITE, AND BLUE. PHILIPPE PETIT WAS HIDDEN IN THE WORLD TRADE CENTER THAT NIGHT, PREPARING HIS TIGHTROPE WALK BETWEEN THE TOWERS THE NEXT MORNING. RELEASED AS PART OF DICK'S PICKS VOLUME 31.

SEPTEMBER 9, 1974 — ALEXANDRA PALACE, LONDON. BUILT IN 1873 AS "THE PEOPLE'S PALACE" — IT BURNED DOWN SIXTEEN DAYS AFTER OPENING AND WAS REBUILT. THE BBC LAUNCHED THE WORLD'S FIRST REGULAR TELEVISION SERVICE FROM THIS BUILDING IN 1936. THE GREAT HALL HAS A VAULTED GLASS CEILING RISING OVER EIGHTY FEET AND AN EIGHT-THOUSAND-PIPE ORGAN — THE LARGEST IN THE COUNTRY. AN EXPLODING GENERATOR DELAYED THE START BY OVER AN HOUR. THE LIGHTING RIG MALFUNCTIONED, LEAVING THE STAGE IN NEAR-TOTAL DARKNESS. PARACHUTES WERE HUNG FROM THE CATHEDRAL CEILING TO IMPROVE THE ACOUSTICS BUT DID NOTHING. THE DEAD PLAYED AFTER TRAFFIC AND ONLY MANAGED ONE SET INSTEAD OF THE USUAL TWO. DRAGGING THE WALL OF SOUND TO EUROPE WAS COSTING A HUNDRED THOUSAND DOLLARS A MONTH — AN EXPENSE THAT WOULD CONTRIBUTE DIRECTLY TO THE HIATUS DECISION A MONTH LATER. THE BRITISH PRESS WAS DIVIDED. STEVE LAKE OF MELODY MAKER PRAISED "THE MOST AMAZING ORGANIC FREEFORM I'VE HEARD FROM ANY BAND." ROBIN DENSELOW OF THE GUARDIAN FOUND IT "COMPETENT BUT FRANKLY DULL." ROBIN KATZ OF SOUNDS FLED WITH A MIGRAINE. FIRST OF THREE NIGHTS. RELEASED AS PART OF DICK'S PICKS VOLUME 7.

SEPTEMBER 10, 1974 — ALEXANDRA PALACE, LONDON. NIGHT TWO. THE CREW HAD SPENT THE DAY ADJUSTING FOR THE GREAT HALL'S ACOUSTICS AND THE IMPROVEMENT SHOWED — "THE SOUND WAS SUPERB AND COMPLETELY DIFFERENT FROM THE WOBBLES OF MONDAY EVENING." TWO COMPLETE SETS THIS TIME, NOT THE TRUNCATED SINGLE SET OF THE NIGHT BEFORE. THE DARK STAR WAS ONE OF ONLY SIX PLAYED ALL YEAR — AND THE ONLY ONE ON THE ENTIRE EUROPEAN TOUR. IT RUNS THIRTY MINUTES AND LEADS INTO A MORNING DEW. BOTH SONGS WOULD ENTER A LONG TWILIGHT AFTER THIS MONTH. THE GREAT HALL'S CATHEDRAL REVERB GAVE THE QUIET PASSAGES A QUALITY THAT NO AMERICAN ARENA COULD REPLICATE — SOMETHING BETWEEN HAUNTING AND HOLY. WHEN THE SHOW ENDED, THE LONDON UNDERGROUND WAS CLOSED, STRANDING FANS IN MUSWELL HILL UNTIL MORNING. GENERALLY CONSIDERED THE BEST NIGHT OF THE LONDON RUN. RELEASED AS PART OF DICK'S PICKS VOLUME 7.

SEPTEMBER 11, 1974 — ALEXANDRA PALACE, LONDON. THE FINAL UK SHOW. THE DEAD WOULD NOT PLAY BRITAIN AGAIN UNTIL 1981. BEFORE THIS NIGHT, THE BAND AND THEIR TRAVELING ENTOURAGE DECIDED TO FLUSH THEIR DRUGS AND TAKE LSD INSTEAD — "A BIT OF LYSERGIC PURIFICATION TO RESET THE LEVELS." NOT EVERYONE COMPLIED. THE BAND'S MANAGER JON MCINTIRE WAS FIRED DURING THIS TOUR. GARCIA AND BILLY KREUTZMANN SAT IN ON THE SEASTONES SET — UNUSUAL, SINCE THOSE ELECTRONIC PERFORMANCES WERE NORMALLY JUST PHIL AND NED LAGIN. THE SEASTONES WERE ESPECIALLY AGGRESSIVE: ONE ATTENDEE'S GIRLFRIEND LOST CONSCIOUSNESS. CANNABIS SMOKE WAS VISIBLE THROUGHOUT THE HALL. PEOPLE HAD BROUGHT TAPE RECORDERS OPENLY — "NO NEED FOR SUBTERFUGE AT ALL." BY WEDNESDAY NIGHT, ONE TAPER HAD UPGRADED TO A BAMBOO-CANE MICROPHONE BOOM. NINETEEN SONGS ACROSS TWO SETS PLUS AN ENCORE. TIPIS WERE SET UP INSIDE THE GREAT HALL. RELEASED AS PART OF DICK'S PICKS VOLUME 7.

SEPTEMBER 18, 1974 — PARC DES EXPOSITIONS, DIJON. AN EXHIBITION HALL IN THE HEART OF BURGUNDY WINE COUNTRY — A TRADE SHOW FACILITY, NOT A CONCERT VENUE. THE DEAD TRAVELED HERE BY BUS THROUGH THE HIGH ALPS AND SWISS VILLAGES, THROUGH RURAL FRANCE WITH ITS CASTLES, AND INTO THE CITY THAT GIVES THE WORLD BOTH ITS FINEST MUSTARD AND ITS GREATEST WINES. WIDELY CONSIDERED THE BEST SHOW OF THE EUROPEAN TOUR. PHIL LESH IS REMARKABLY HIGH IN THE MIX — "ON FIRE ALL NIGHT, AND YOU CAN HEAR EVERY CRAZY NOTE." THE THIRD SET INCLUDES THE FIRST CAUTION JAM SINCE 1969 — A CALLBACK TO THE BAND'S EARLIEST PSYCHEDELIC ORIGINS. ONE REVIEWER DETECTED A RIFF IN THE PLAYING IN THE BAND THAT MAY BE A PROTOTYPE OF "SHAKEDOWN STREET," FOUR YEARS BEFORE THE SONG EXISTED. PHIL ATTEMPTED TO COMMUNICATE IN FRENCH. MOST TAPES IN CIRCULATION DERIVE FROM A FRENCH PIRATE LP THAT SCRAMBLED THE SEQUENCE OF SONGS — IF YOU'RE CONFUSED BY THE TRACK ORDER, THAT'S WHY. SELECTED FOR THE 30 TRIPS AROUND THE SUN BOX SET AS THE 1974 ENTRY.

SEPTEMBER 20, 1974 — PALAIS DES SPORTS, PARIS. THE BUILDING HAS A BUCKMINSTER FULLER DOME — ELEVEN HUNDRED DIAMOND-SHAPED ALUMINUM PANELS FORMING THE LARGEST SELF-SUPPORTING LIGHT-ALLOY DOME IN THE WORLD WHEN IT WAS BUILT. IT STANDS ON THE SITE WHERE FRANCE HOSTED ITS FIRST INTERNATIONAL ROCK FESTIVAL IN 1961 — JOHNNY HALLYDAY AND LES CHAUSSETTES NOIRES MARKING THE BEGINNING OF ROCK AND ROLL IN FRANCE. THE PALAIS HELD ABOUT FORTY-SIX HUNDRED — ROUGHLY A THIRD THE SIZE OF A TYPICAL AMERICAN ARENA DATE. THE DEAD WOULD NOT RETURN TO EUROPE UNTIL 1981. BROKEDOWN PALACE APPEARED IN THE SECOND SET — ONE OF ONLY FOUR PERFORMANCES ALL YEAR. THE TRUCKIN' > EYES OF THE WORLD PAIRING CLOSED THE SECOND SET WITH REAL MOMENTUM. BUT MOST OF THE ARCHIVAL DISCUSSION OF THIS SHOW FOCUSES ON SOMETHING DARKER: THE BUILDING WAS CONSTRUCTED ON THE SITE OF THE VELODROME D'HIVER, WHERE VICHY POLICE ROUNDED UP OVER THIRTEEN THOUSAND JEWS — INCLUDING FOUR THOUSAND CHILDREN — IN JULY 1942 FOR DEPORTATION TO AUSCHWITZ. FRANCE DENIED THE CRIME FOR THIRTY-SEVEN YEARS.

SEPTEMBER 21, 1974 — PALAIS DES SPORTS, PARIS. THE FINAL SHOW OF THE EUROPEAN TOUR. THE DEAD WOULD NOT PLAY OUTSIDE NORTH AMERICA AGAIN FOR SEVEN YEARS. THE RECORDING HAS A SIGNIFICANT PROBLEM: BOB WEIR'S GUITAR IS ALMOST COMPLETELY ABSENT FROM THE MIX, MAKING THE BAND SOUND, AS ONE WRITER PUT IT, "LIKE A VERY HOLLOW VERSION OF THE JERRY GARCIA BAND WITH A BETTER BASS PLAYER." THE FIRST SET IS STRONG DESPITE THE MIX — KEITH AND PHIL CARRY THE WEIGHT. THE MORNING DEW IN THE THIRD SET IS THE EMOTIONAL PEAK, THOUGH IT MAY RUN LONGER THAN THE MATERIAL CAN SUSTAIN. THE TAPE OF SET ONE IS NOT IN CIRCULATION; WHAT SURVIVES BEGINS WITH THE SEASTONES AND RUNS THROUGH THE END. WITHIN WEEKS, THE BAND WOULD PLAY THEIR FAREWELL CONCERTS AT WINTERLAND AND GO SILENT FOR NEARLY TWO YEARS. THE HUNDRED-THOUSAND-DOLLAR-A-MONTH COST OF MOVING THE WALL OF SOUND ACROSS CONTINENTS HAD BROUGHT THEM TO THE EDGE.

OCTOBER 16, 1974 — WINTERLAND ARENA, SAN FRANCISCO. FAREWELL NIGHT ONE. FIVE NIGHTS, SIX CAMERAS, TWO SIXTEEN-TRACK TAPE MACHINES — THE BAND WAS FILMING WHAT WOULD BECOME THE GRATEFUL DEAD MOVIE. THE ATMOSPHERE WAS CELEBRATORY, NOT MOROSE. THE HAIGHT-ASHBURY FREE MEDICAL CLINIC MAINTAINED ITS USUAL OFFICE AT THE BACK OF THE HALL. HELL'S ANGELS WERE OUT IN FORCE. IT WAS BOB WEIR'S TWENTY-SEVENTH BIRTHDAY. PHIL LESH SANG "HAPPY BIRTHDAY." THE PLAYING IN THE BAND THAT CLOSES THE FIRST SET WAS USED IN THE FILM AND HAS BECOME ONE OF THE MOST-SEEN PERFORMANCES IN THE DEAD'S VISUAL ARCHIVE. GARCIA CUED THE ENDING MULTIPLE TIMES BUT THE OTHERS WEREN'T READY — THE SONG KEPT GOING. A TECHNICAL PROBLEM HAUNTED THE RUN: WEIR'S GUITAR MIC FELL OVER DURING THE FIRST SET AND THE SIGNAL LOSS CONTINUED THROUGH BOTH SETS. BETTY CANTOR-JACKSON, THE BAND'S REGULAR RECORDING ENGINEER, HAD RECENTLY HAD A CHILD; BILLY WOLF WAS BEHIND THE BOARD INSTEAD. SHE LATER SAID: "THE TAPES WERE PRETTY AWFUL." RELEASED ON THE GRATEFUL DEAD MOVIE SOUNDTRACK.

OCTOBER 17, 1974 — WINTERLAND ARENA. FAREWELL NIGHT TWO. THE PIANO LID FELL MID-SONG DURING PROMISED LAND, CAUSING A BUZZY TECHNICAL ISSUE THAT PERSISTED. THE SCARLET BEGONIAS WAS THE BEST OF THREE PLAYED THAT WEEK, WITH A NOTABLE DONNA JEAN SCAT SOLO. A LIVE MICROPHONE CAPTURED WHAT SOUNDS LIKE A TODDLER THROWING A TANTRUM ABOUT FOUR MINUTES INTO THE SEASTONES SET. THE SECOND SET IS THE REASON TO LISTEN: HE'S GONE STAYS MELLOW UNTIL LESH "ABRUPTLY INTENSIFIES" INTO THE OTHER ONE, WHICH RUNS FOR OVER THIRTY MINUTES AND CONTAINS THE ONLY FULL SPACE SECTION OF THE ENTIRE RUN — A "SCREECHING MELTDOWN." SPANISH JAM AND MIND LEFT BODY JAM APPEAR INSIDE THE SUITE BEFORE IT RESOLVES INTO STELLA BLUE. ONE ANALYST CALLS THIS "THE BEST SHOW OF THE FAREWELL RUN" — A QUIET SLEEPER OVERSHADOWED BY THE DARK STAR NIGHT THAT FOLLOWED. RELEASED ON STEAL YOUR FACE AND THE GRATEFUL DEAD MOVIE SOUNDTRACK.

OCTOBER 18, 1974 — WINTERLAND ARENA. FAREWELL NIGHT THREE. THIS IS THE NIGHT. THE ONLY DARK STAR OF THE FIVE-SHOW RUN — AND ONE OF ONLY SIX IN ALL OF 1974. THE SEASTONES SET WAS THE DEEPEST AND MOST PATIENT OF THE WEEK. NED LAGIN DISPERSED IMPATIENT AUDIENCE CLAPPERS WITH A MASSIVE SYNTHESIZER WASH AROUND THE EIGHT-MINUTE MARK. GARCIA ENTERED ABOUT FOURTEEN MINUTES IN, AND THE MUSIC BECAME WHAT ONE LISTENER CALLED "A UNIQUE BLOB OF ACID-SATURATED '74 GRATEFUL DEAD" — LACKING THE "COCAINE-EDGED SHARPNESS" OF MUCH OF THE YEAR. AFTER A TUNING CHANGE, GARCIA QUIETLY BEGAN MORNING DEW — SLOW, BEAUTIFUL, AND NOW IMMORTALIZED IN THE FILM. IT WAS ALSO THE FINAL COMPLETE WEATHER REPORT SUITE. THE PRELUDE AND PART ONE WERE NEVER PLAYED AGAIN AFTER THIS NIGHT. THE DEAD HAD ONE OF THEIR MOST COMPLEX COMPOSITIONS AND SIMPLY WALKED AWAY FROM HALF OF IT. RELEASED ON THE GRATEFUL DEAD MOVIE SOUNDTRACK.

OCTOBER 19, 1974 — WINTERLAND ARENA. FAREWELL NIGHT FOUR. THE PENULTIMATE SHOW HAD A DIFFERENT ENERGY — THE BAND COULD PLAY WITHOUT THE PRESSURE OF THE ACTUAL LAST NIGHT. THE EYES OF THE WORLD IS CONSIDERED ONE OF THE FINEST THE BAND EVER PLAYED — IT TAKES UP "ABOUT HALF THE GRATEFUL DEAD MOVIE." THE SEASTONES OPENED WITH AN OCEAN RECORDING AND A DRUM MACHINE. NED LAGIN WAS USING AN ARP 2600 SYNTHESIZER WITH A SEQUENCER "CONTROLLED BY A PROGRAM THAT SENDS IT RANDOM OR NOT RANDOM VALUES THAT EVOLVE OVER TIME." AN EYEWITNESS SAID IT WAS "SO LOUD IT WAS SCARY — FELT LIKE SOUND WAS EMANATING FROM INSIDE YOUR SKULL." TOMORROW IS FOREVER MADE ITS LAST-EVER APPEARANCE. A SURPRISE BLACK PETER SHOWED UP — ONE OF ONLY THREE THAT YEAR. GARCIA LISTENED TO DOLLY PARTON IN A PLAYBOY INTERVIEW AROUND THIS TIME AND WAS DEEPLY MOVED; THE COUNTRY SONGS IN THE SETLIST CARRIED EXTRA WEIGHT. RELEASED ON STEAL YOUR FACE AND THE GRATEFUL DEAD MOVIE SOUNDTRACK.

OCTOBER 20, 1974 — WINTERLAND ARENA, SAN FRANCISCO. BILLED AS "THE LAST ONE." TICKETS WERE FIVE DOLLARS. THEY WERE STAMPED WITH THE WORDS.
